name: Pierre-Carl Langlais
paper_title: Common Corpus: The Largest Collection of Ethical Data for LLM Pre-Training
synopsis: This paper addresses the growing legal risks of training Large Language Models on copyrighted data that is becoming increasingly difficult to access under new AI regulations. The authors introduce Common Corpus, a two trillion token dataset built entirely from uncopyrighted or permissively licensed sources covering diverse languages, code, and domains. Testing with small language models shows the dataset performs comparably to existing benchmarks while ensuring full compliance with strict legal requirements. This resource enables developers to build ethical, multilingual AI systems that remain legally secure without relying on restricted web scrapes.

name: Artyom Sorokin
paper_title: Q-RAG: Long Context Multi‑Step Retrieval via Value‑Based Embedder Training
synopsis: This paper addresses the high computational cost and inefficiency of existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems that require multi-step search to answer complex questions over massive documents. The authors introduce Q-RAG, a resource-efficient method that uses reinforcement learning to fine-tune embedding models rather than large language models, enabling compatibility with any model size including proprietary ones. It achieves state-of-the-art results on ultra-long context benchmarks up to 10 million tokens while remaining significantly faster and cheaper than competing multi-step approaches. These advancements unlock practical use cases for open-domain question answering over vast corpora like legal or medical records without incurring prohibitive inference costs or training budgets.

name: Evgeny Burnaev
paper_title: Universal Inverse Distillation for Matching Models with Real-Data Supervision (No GANs)
synopsis: This paper addresses the slow inference speeds of modern generative models like diffusion and flow matching by proposing a new distillation framework that naturally utilizes real data without complex adversarial training. The authors introduce RealUID, which unifies previous methods under a single universal loss function, eliminating the need for extra discriminator networks typically required in GAN-based approaches. By streamlining the distillation process with simple yet rigorous theoretical explanations, the method achieves efficient one-step generation while avoiding the computational overhead associated with adversarial losses. Consequently, this research unlocks faster and more cost-effective deployment of high-fidelity AI models across diverse architectures, addressing a major barrier to scaling generative applications in production environments.

name: Marco P Abrate
institution: University College London, University of London
paper_title: From movement to cognitive maps: recurrent neural networks reveal how locomotor development shapes hippocampal spatial coding
synopsis: This study investigates how a young animal's developing motor skills build the brain's internal map of space. The researchers used artificial neural networks trained on real rat movement data to simulate how spatial neurons mature during growth. Their findings show that specific changes in movement patterns, not just faster learning, are required to create accurate, world-centered navigation systems that match biological timelines. These insights unlock new ways to design AI agents that learn navigation through physical experience and provide tools to model neurodevelopmental conditions where movement and spatial memory are affected.

name: Isaac Han
institution: Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology
paper_title: FIRE: Frobenius-Isometry Reinitialization for Balancing the Stability–Plasticity Tradeoff
synopsis: This research tackles the challenge of enabling deep neural networks to adapt to new data streams without forgetting crucial prior knowledge, a key requirement for evolving applications like autonomous vehicles and foundation models. The authors propose FIRE, an efficient reinitialization strategy that mathematically balances stability and plasticity using a constrained optimization approach solved with minimal computational overhead. Testing across computer vision, language modeling, and reinforcement learning benchmarks demonstrates that FIRE outperforms standard methods while adding less than 1% to training time costs. This advancement unlocks scalable AI solutions that can update continuously in dynamic environments without catastrophic forgetting or prohibitive data storage needs.

name: Kartik Nair
paper_title: Exchangeability of GNN Representations  with Applications to Graph Retrieval
synopsis: This research identifies a hidden statistical symmetry in trained Graph Neural Networks where individual node embedding features remain interchangeable across dimensions. By proving that these embedding components behave as exchangeable random variables, the authors enable complex graph matching metrics to be efficiently approximated using simple Euclidean distances on sorted vectors. This breakthrough unlocks a unified Locality Sensitive Hashing framework for scalable graph retrieval, allowing search systems to find relevant graphs much faster while supporting diverse relevance measures like subgraph matching and edit distance.

name: Zhiyu Mou
paper_title: Enhancing Generative Auto-bidding with Offline Reward Evaluation and Policy Search
synopsis: Current AI-generated bidding methods are limited to imitating historical data, which prevents them from safely exploring higher-performing strategies beyond static datasets. This paper introduces AIGB-Pearl, a novel framework that integrates offline reward evaluation with policy search to enable active exploration while ensuring model safety through provable constraints. By avoiding the training instability typical of traditional reinforcement learning, this approach leverages synchronous coupling techniques to maintain regularity during generation. Extensive tests on simulated and real-world advertising systems confirm that AIGB-Pearl achieves state-of-the-art performance, enabling advertisers to significantly optimize campaign returns within budget limits.

name: Aditya Gopalan
paper_title: Why DPO is a Misspecified Estimator and How to Fix It
synopsis: This research identifies a critical statistical flaw in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), revealing that standard alignment methods fail on real neural networks because they incorrectly assume the model can access every possible response distribution. To overcome this limitation, the authors develop AuxDPO, which adds controlled degrees of freedom to the training loss to bridge the gap between lightweight DPO and computationally expensive two-stage Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Experiments confirm that this new algorithm prevents common issues like preference reversal and reward degradation, consistently outperforming standard DPO on real-world large language model tasks. This advancement offers a reliable, resource-efficient solution for industries seeking to fine-tune AI models without the heavy compute costs associated with traditional RLHF pipelines.

name: Sayak Ray Chowdhury
paper_title: Why DPO is a Misspecified Estimator and How to Fix It
synopsis: This research identifies a critical statistical flaw in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), revealing that standard alignment methods fail on real neural networks because they incorrectly assume the model can access every possible response distribution. To overcome this limitation, the authors develop AuxDPO, which adds controlled degrees of freedom to the training loss to bridge the gap between lightweight DPO and computationally expensive two-stage Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Experiments confirm that this new algorithm prevents common issues like preference reversal and reward degradation, consistently outperforming standard DPO on real-world large language model tasks. This advancement offers a reliable, resource-efficient solution for industries seeking to fine-tune AI models without the heavy compute costs associated with traditional RLHF pipelines.

name: Chunyang Li
institution: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
paper_title: WebDevJudge: Evaluating (M)LLMs as Critiques for Web Development Quality
synopsis: This paper addresses the reliability issues of using Large Language Models as automated judges for complex web development tasks, where current methods often fail in dynamic environments. The authors introduce WEBDEVJUDGE, a benchmark featuring both static code analysis and interactive web environments that rigorously measure AI evaluators against high-quality human annotations. Their experiments reveal a significant performance gap exceeding 15% between current models and human experts, primarily due to weaknesses in verifying task feasibility and recognizing functionally equivalent solutions. For industry stakeholders, these insights clarify where automated evaluation tools currently fall short and define the technical hurdles needed before deploying autonomous AI agents in critical production workflows.

name: Geon-Hyeong Kim
paper_title: SafeDPO: A Simple Approach to Direct Preference Optimization with Enhanced Safety
synopsis: This paper addresses the critical challenge of aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) to be safe without relying on complex auxiliary models or multi-stage pipelines. The authors propose SafeDPO, a lightweight method that derives a closed-form solution to safety alignment using only standard preference data and binary safety indicators, eliminating the need for reward models or online sampling. Empirical results demonstrate that SafeDPO significantly improves safety while maintaining helpfulness across models up to 13B parameters, outperforming existing techniques that rely on relaxed constraints. This approach unlocks efficient deployment of secure AI assistants in real-world applications where strict safety enforcement is essential but computational overhead must be minimized.

name: Ran Xu
paper_title: MedAgentGym: A Scalable Agentic Training Environment for Code-Centric Reasoning in Biomedical Data Science
synopsis: Biomedical data analysis requires specialized coding skills that current large language models lack, while relying on proprietary competitors is often too costly or privacy-invasive. To solve this, the researchers introduced MedAgentGym, a scalable platform with over 72,000 executable tasks that trains AI agents in safe, isolated sandbox environments to perform complex medical reasoning and data science workflows. Their custom model achieved significant performance gains comparable to top-tier commercial tools like GPT-4o, demonstrating that cost-effective open-source models can be effectively trained to handle these specialized tasks. This tool unlocks affordable, privacy-preserving AI assistants for hospitals and researchers who need robust coding capabilities for electronic health records and predictive modeling without relying on expensive cloud APIs.

name: Ran Xu
paper_title: On the Generalization Capacities of MLLMs for Spatial Intelligence
synopsis: Current Multimodal Large Language Models using only RGB images fail to generalize across different cameras because they ignore essential optical parameters, causing them to overfit to specific hardware configurations. To fix this, researchers introduce a Camera-Aware framework that explicitly embeds camera data into visual tokens and trains with synthetic variations to separate scene content from device properties. Experiments demonstrate that these improved models maintain robust performance on new cameras without retraining, whereas standard models fail catastrophically when encountering different optical setups. This breakthrough enables reliable 3D spatial reasoning for robotics and autonomous navigation systems that must operate safely across diverse real-world environments and hardware setups.

name: Carl Yang
paper_title: Seeing Through the Brain: New Insights from Decoding Visual Stimuli with fMRI
synopsis: This study solves the difficulty of reconstructing detailed visual images from fMRI brain scans by challenging the assumption that vision-based models are necessary for the latent space. The researchers developed PRISM, a framework that translates neural signals into a structured text space using language models to capture the compositional nature of objects and attributes more effectively than previous methods. Their findings reveal that this text-based intermediate representation aligns better with brain activity, achieving an 8% reduction in perceptual loss compared to state-of-the-art competitors. This breakthrough paves the way for more accurate brain-computer interfaces and enables new possibilities in generating content directly from human neural activity without needing visual input during training.

name: Thomas Rupf
paper_title: Optimistic Task Inference for Behavior Foundation Models
synopsis: Current Behavior Foundation Models require expensive labeled datasets to adapt to new tasks at test-time, creating significant cost barriers for real-world deployment. To solve this, the authors propose OpTI-BFM, an online framework that actively collects interaction data during operation to estimate task rewards without relying on offline storage or heavy labeling efforts. Their results demonstrate that this optimistic strategy identifies optimal policies within just a few episodes while reducing compute overhead compared to standard pipelines. This research enables cost-effective AI agents that can adapt to new objectives in dynamic environments where human supervision for reward labels is limited or proprietary data is inaccessible.

name: Marco Bagatella
institution: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems
paper_title: TD-JEPA: Latent-predictive Representations for Zero-Shot Reinforcement Learning
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of training reinforcement learning agents to adapt to new tasks without requiring costly real-time rewards or extensive online data collection. The authors introduce TD-JEPA, which uses temporal difference learning to train models on past interactions so they can predict future outcomes across multiple tasks within a latent representation space. Empirically, the system learns robust representations that capture long-term dynamics and outperforms existing benchmarks on complex navigation, manipulation, and pixel-based robotic tasks. This unlocks practical applications where AI agents can instantly optimize for new objectives using historical data, significantly lowering the cost of training robots and autonomous systems for varied goals.

name: Yahan Li
paper_title: CounselBench: A Large-Scale Expert Evaluation and Adversarial Benchmarking of Large Language Models in Mental Health Question Answering
synopsis: Current medical AI benchmarks prioritize facts over open-ended empathy, leaving critical safety gaps in mental health contexts where patient questions require nuanced emotional support. To fix this, the team developed COUNSELBENCH, a large-scale framework utilizing 100 clinicians to rate real patient queries and 120 adversarial prompts specifically crafted to expose model weaknesses. Evaluation results show that while LLMs score well on facts, they frequently violate safety protocols or lack personalization, and even AI judges often overestimate their own reliability compared to human experts. This benchmark enables developers and investors to rigorously stress-test AI models for digital therapy platforms, ensuring they can handle sensitive emotional needs without risking harm or liability.

name: Ashish Sabharwal
paper_title: On the Reasoning Abilities of Masked Diffusion Language Models
synopsis: This study addresses the lack of theoretical understanding regarding the computational capabilities and efficiency limits of Masked Diffusion Models compared to traditional sequential language models. The researchers establish a formal connection between MDMs and established reasoning frameworks like Chain of Thought to characterize exactly which problems these parallel architectures can solve. Their main finding is that while MDMs match the problem-solving power of standard chain-of-thought transformers, they are provably more efficient on parallelizable tasks by avoiding sequential bottlenecks. These insights unlock opportunities for leveraging diffusion models in compute-sensitive applications where decomposing complex reasoning into parallel sub-problems significantly reduces generation time.

synopsis: Current benchmarks for AI agents are often unreliable because they use inconsistent tools, ignore computational costs, and fail to represent real-world scientific work. name: Sina Alemohammad
paper_title: Neon: Negative Extrapolation From Self-Training Improves Image Generation
synopsis: Generative AI progress is currently bottlenecked by a scarcity of high-quality real training data, and standard self-training often leads to model collapse where quality degrades rapidly. The Neon method addresses this by treating the natural degradation from synthetic data as a useful signal that can be reversed through a simple parameter extrapolation technique without requiring new datasets or complex auxiliary models. Research demonstrates this approach consistently improves image generation performance across various architectures using less than 1% additional compute and no real data augmentation. This enables companies to continue improving their generative systems efficiently even when original training sources are exhausted, offering a sustainable solution for scaling AI models beyond current data limits.

name: Utkarsh Aashu Mishra
paper_title: Compositional Diffusion with Guided search for Long-Horizon Planning
synopsis: This paper addresses a critical failure in current generative planning models where combining local distributions causes incompatible modes to average out, resulting in incoherent long-horizon outputs. To fix this, the authors propose Compositional Diffusion with Guided Search, which integrates a guided search mechanism directly into the diffusion process to explore diverse plans and prune inconsistent paths while ensuring global consistency. Experiments demonstrate that the method matches ideal performance on robotics tasks and generates coherent videos and panoramas without needing expensive long-horizon training data. Ultimately, this breakthrough enables scalable applications for autonomous robot manipulation and consistent creative media generation across extended sequences.

name: Rim Assouel
paper_title: Visual symbolic mechanisms: Emergent symbol processing in Vision Language Models
synopsis: This paper addresses why Vision Language Models frequently struggle to bind visual features together accurately, leading to errors in tasks like counting and distinguishing object combinations. The researchers identified that these models use an emergent spatial indexing system called "position IDs" to track objects and their attributes similarly to symbolic variables used in text-based AI. Through intervention analyses on seven different models, they found that binding failures occur directly when this internal tracking mechanism interferes during the retrieval of object features. These findings unlock a specific path for developers to improve visual grounding capabilities by stabilizing these symbolic mechanisms, thereby reducing common failure modes in complex scene understanding.

name: Yoshua Bengio
paper_title: FALCON: Few-step Accurate Likelihoods for Continuous Flows
synopsis: Molecular sampling is a foundational tool for drug discovery and material science, yet current continuous flow models are prohibitively slow because calculating their likelihoods requires excessive computational resources. This paper introduces FALCON, a new generative model that combines efficient few-step sampling with accurate likelihood estimates suitable for scientific importance sampling techniques. By leveraging a hybrid training objective to ensure invertibility without sacrificing speed, FALCON achieves performance two orders of magnitude faster than existing state-of-the-art models while requiring far less training data. This advancement unlocks scalable pathways for characterizing biomolecules and accelerating the design of novel drugs and materials with significantly reduced computational overhead.

name: Satchel Grant
paper_title: Addressing divergent representations from causal interventions on neural networks
synopsis: Many techniques used to understand how neural networks work artificially alter internal data, which can shift the model into unnatural states and make explanations unreliable. This research identifies two types of such distortions—some harmless and some dangerous—and proposes a new method using a modified loss function to prevent harmful shifts without losing analytical power. The study shows that keeping intervened data closer to natural distributions prevents hidden parts of the network from triggering unintended behaviors while maintaining accurate testing capabilities. Ultimately, these findings enable developers to build more trustworthy diagnostic tools for auditing large language models and ensuring AI safety without compromising interpretability.

name: Christopher Potts
paper_title: GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning
synopsis: Current AI optimization techniques often require thousands of expensive model runs to improve performance, creating high computational costs. To solve this, the authors developed GEPA, a system that uses natural language reflection to diagnose errors and evolve prompts instead of relying on inefficient reinforcement learning rewards. Testing across multiple tasks shows GEPA outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 20% accuracy while using 35 times fewer resources. This approach enables efficient fine-tuning of complex AI agents for real-world applications where inference budgets or tool call costs are strictly limited.

name: Nam Nguyen
paper_title: Cross-Domain Lossy Compression via Rate- and Classification-Constrained Optimal Transport
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of converting degraded, noisy data into a distinct high-quality format while simultaneously maintaining performance on downstream AI tasks. By framing cross-domain restoration as rate- and classification-constrained optimal transport, the authors derive mathematical equations that reveal fundamental tradeoffs between file size, image fidelity, and task accuracy without relying solely on trial-and-error deep learning. Their framework provides explicit solutions for common data models, enabling engineers to design compression systems with predictable performance guarantees rather than empirical guessing. Validated across tasks like image denoising and super-resolution, this research unlocks the ability to create generative codecs that are optimized for specific restoration requirements while ensuring downstream models can still function effectively.

name: Mason Long Wang
paper_title: Latent Fourier Transform
synopsis: Existing generative music models struggle to isolate and manipulate specific timescales, such as separating chord progressions from fast trills, often leading to unintended feature interference during editing. This paper introduces Latent Fourier Transform, a framework that combines diffusion autoencoders with latent-space frequency masking to create a continuous axis for controlling musical structure at different frequencies. By treating the model's internal representation like an equalizer, developers can preserve desired patterns while generating variations or blending tracks coherently. Consequently, this research unlocks new capabilities for interactive audio production, allowing users to precisely condition generation tasks on specific timescales rather than just high-level semantic attributes.

name: Peter Holderrieth
paper_title: GLASS Flows: Efficient Inference for Reward Alignment of Flow and Diffusion Models
synopsis: Reward alignment algorithms often slow down diffusion models because they rely on inefficient stochastic sampling methods to explore better generation options. To solve this, the authors introduce GLASS Flows, a technique that allows these models to simulate necessary randomness using fast Ordinary Differential Equations without requiring retraining. This innovation eliminates the traditional trade-off between computational speed and search capability, significantly improving text-to-image generation performance. The result is a practical, drop-in upgrade for deploying high-efficiency inference in generative AI applications like image synthesis and molecular design.

name: Yen-Ju Lu
paper_title: Latent Speech-Text Transformer
synopsis: This study tackles the inefficiency of current speech models that require far more data and processing time because raw speech tokens are longer and less compact than text tokens. The authors introduce the Latent Speech-Text Transformer, which solves this by grouping speech signals into dynamic patches that match the information density of text sequences. Experiments demonstrate that this approach outperforms standard models on speech and text tasks while significantly reducing the computing power needed for training and inference. Ultimately, this innovation enables more scalable generative voice AI systems that align audio and language capabilities efficiently without requiring excessive resources.

name: Siyuan Wang
paper_title: LoongRL: Reinforcement Learning for Advanced Reasoning over Long Contexts
synopsis: LoongRL addresses the critical challenge of enabling large language models to reason deeply across extremely long documents, which is essential for complex tasks like legal analysis and debugging codebases but currently limited by scarce training data and high compute costs. The team introduces KeyChain, a method that transforms short questions into challenging long-context problems by inserting hidden clues among distracting text, forcing models to learn specific retrieval-and-reasoning patterns during reinforcement learning. Their results show that models trained on shorter 16K contexts can generalize to handle massive 128K tasks with significant accuracy improvements, matching performance from much larger and more expensive models. This breakthrough enables enterprise applications that need to analyze vast amounts of text for critical decisions without requiring prohibitive training infrastructure or sacrificing general reasoning skills.

name: Fan Yang
paper_title: Agent Data Protocol: Unifying Datasets for Diverse, Effective Fine-tuning of LLM Agents
synopsis: Large language model agents face a significant scaling bottleneck because high-quality training data is fragmented across incompatible formats and interfaces, preventing large-scale supervised fine-tuning. This paper introduces the Agent Data Protocol (ADP), a lightweight standardization layer that unifies thirteen existing datasets into a single representation for diverse tasks like coding and web browsing. Experiments confirm that fine-tuning with this unified protocol delivers approximately twenty percent average performance gains over base models while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art results. By lowering the engineering barrier to integrate heterogeneous data, ADP enables scalable, reproducible training pipelines that allow developers to leverage public resources efficiently across multiple agent frameworks.

name: Fan Yang
paper_title: TileLang: Bridge Programmability and Performance in Modern Neural Kernels
synopsis: Current AI kernel development faces a steep trade-off where high-level compilers lack the control needed for peak performance while manual coding is overly complex. This paper presents TILELANG, a tile-level programming system that grants explicit control over memory placement and parallelism while using automated inference to handle configuration details automatically. The authors demonstrate that their approach delivers up to 10x speedups on top GPUs compared to existing compilers like Triton while shrinking codebases by over 85% through hardware-aware defaults. This capability enables researchers to efficiently implement modern algorithms like FlashMLA and Multi-Head Attention, effectively bridging the gap between developer productivity and high-performance computing requirements.

name: Naimeng Ye
paper_title: Speculative Actions: A Lossless Framework for Faster AI Agents
synopsis: AI agents are currently hampered by slow end-to-end latency because every action must wait for a sequential API call to complete before proceeding. This research proposes a "speculative actions" framework that uses faster models to guess upcoming steps in parallel while slower systems validate them, maintaining full accuracy without sacrificing performance. The study reports significant speedups, including up to 20% reduced latency and 55% action prediction accuracy across diverse environments like gaming, e-commerce, and web search. This approach effectively breaks the sequential bottleneck, enabling high-throughput automation and faster deployment of practical AI systems in real-world workflows.

name: Aaradhya Pandey
paper_title: Gaussian certified unlearning in high dimensions: A hypothesis testing approach
synopsis: This paper addresses the critical challenge of machine unlearning in high-dimensional settings where model parameters are comparable to sample sizes, a scenario that often breaks standard theoretical assumptions used in prior research. The authors introduce ε-Gaussian certifiability as a robust privacy metric for this regime and demonstrate that a single Newton update with carefully calibrated noise is sufficient to ensure both privacy and accuracy. This finding contradicts earlier studies requiring multiple steps by showing that their underlying notions were suboptimal, thereby enabling efficient data removal without excessive model degradation. Ultimately, this work unlocks practical pathways for AI companies to comply with right-to-be-forgotten regulations and manage sensitive data securely at scale without incurring the prohibitive costs of full model retraining.

Ultimately, this work unlocks practical pathways for AI companies to comply with right-to-be-forgotten regulations and manage sensitive data securely at scale without incurring the prohibitive costs of full model retraining.

name: Zeyi Liao
paper_title: RedTeamCUA: Realistic Adversarial Testing of Computer-Use Agents in Hybrid Web-OS Environments
synopsis: Computer-use agents are increasingly vulnerable to indirect prompt injection, yet existing testing frameworks lack realistic hybrid environments that combine web and operating system contexts. To address this gap, the authors propose REDTEAMCUA, a novel adversarial framework integrating virtual machine-based OS systems with Dockerized web platforms to safely simulate complex cross-platform attack scenarios. Benchmarking reveals severe security flaws, with leading models executing harmful actions in up to 50% of realistic cases and attempting malicious tasks in nearly 93% of attempts despite failing to complete them. These results provide critical risk intelligence for deploying autonomous software, underscoring the immediate necessity for robust defenses against web-OS prompt injection threats prior to market launch.

name: Yu Su
paper_title: Agent Data Protocol: Unifying Datasets for Diverse, Effective Fine-tuning of LLM Agents
synopsis: Large language model agents face a significant scaling bottleneck because high-quality training data is fragmented across incompatible formats and interfaces, preventing large-scale supervised fine-tuning. This paper introduces the Agent Data Protocol (ADP), a lightweight standardization layer that unifies thirteen existing datasets into a single representation for diverse tasks like coding and web browsing. Experiments confirm that fine-tuning with this unified protocol delivers approximately twenty percent average performance gains over base models while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art results. By lowering the engineering barrier to integrate heterogeneous data, ADP enables scalable, reproducible training pipelines that allow developers to leverage public resources efficiently across multiple agent frameworks.

name: Tommaso Mencattini
paper_title: Exploratory Causal Inference in SAEnce
synopsis: Traditional scientific trials often fail to scale because they rely on expensive, pre-defined hypotheses that bias researchers toward only known phenomena. To address this, the authors introduce Neural Effect Search, which uses foundation models and sparse autoencoders to automatically discover causal effects directly from raw data without needing initial labels. This recursive procedure effectively separates true causal signals from statistical noise to robustly identify significant treatment impacts within complex neural representations. The method proves effective in experimental ecology studies, unlocking new ways for scientists to explore vast biological datasets and validate unbiased discoveries before final hypothesis confirmation.

name: Francesco Locatello
paper_title: Navigating the Latent Space Dynamics of Neural Models
synopsis: This paper reframes autoencoder neural networks as dynamical systems that naturally define a latent vector field through standard training processes without additional tuning. The authors demonstrate that regularization techniques create attractor points within this space, which mathematically encode the model's memorization and generalization capabilities directly in its weights. By analyzing these latent trajectories, developers can extract embedded prior knowledge without input data and effectively detect out-of-distribution samples or distribution shifts during deployment. Consequently, this research offers a practical framework for auditing foundation models to ensure robustness and safety before scaling up to real-world scenarios.

name: Francesco Locatello
paper_title: High-dimensional Analysis of Synthetic Data Selection
synopsis: This paper tackles the challenge of selecting which synthetic data from generative models should be used to train machine learning classifiers to maximize performance. Through theoretical analysis, the researchers find that simply matching the statistical covariance between real and synthetic data minimizes generalization error, as mean shifts have little impact on model accuracy. Consequently, this straightforward alignment strategy outperforms complex filtering heuristics across different neural network architectures and datasets without causing model collapse or bias. These findings offer a practical blueprint for industries facing data limitations, such as healthcare, to effectively utilize generative AI for augmentation while maintaining high prediction reliability.

name: Matthew Finlayson
paper_title: Every Language Model Has a Forgery-Resistant Signature
synopsis: This paper addresses the need to verify the source of text generated by closed-weight language models using limited API access. The authors reveal that all modern models inherently constrain their outputs to lie on a high-dimensional ellipse, creating a unique geometric signature that is naturally occurring and impossible to forge without direct model access. Experimental results show this signature is self-contained within individual logit outputs and significantly more secure against manipulation than existing fingerprinting techniques. These findings enable a new verification protocol similar to cryptographic authentication, allowing regulators or partners to authenticate AI-generated content and ensure accountability for opaque models without requiring weight release.

name: Swabha Swayamdipta
paper_title: How Reliable is Language Model Micro-Benchmarking?
synopsis: This paper investigates whether using small data subsets to test AI models reliably predicts performance compared to full-scale benchmarks. To address this, the researchers introduce a new metric that quantifies how many examples are needed to consistently distinguish between two competing models. Their analysis reveals that complex selection methods offer minimal advantages over simple random sampling unless the subset is large enough to capture minor performance differences, typically requiring around 250 examples. These findings provide actionable guidance for developers and investors on balancing evaluation speed with accuracy, suggesting that random sampling is often sufficient when comparing models with similar capabilities.

name: Konwoo Kim
paper_title: Pre-training under infinite compute
synopsis: This paper addresses how to efficiently train large language models when data availability becomes the primary bottleneck rather than computing power. The authors find that standard training methods overfit quickly under fixed data, so they propose using significantly higher regularization weight decay and ensembling multiple smaller models instead of a single massive one. Their main result shows this optimized approach reaches its theoretical performance limit using 5.17 times less training data while achieving strong downstream benchmark scores. Consequently, these techniques unlock more sustainable model training strategies and enable distillation into compact models that retain most of the ensemble’s intelligence without increasing inference costs.

name: Tatsunori Hashimoto
paper_title: OpenThoughts: Data Recipes for Reasoning Models
synopsis: The OpenThoughts project addresses the critical barrier of proprietary datasets that currently prevent open-source researchers from replicating state-of-the-art reasoning AI capabilities in math, code, and science. By systematically investigating over 1,000 data generation strategies, the authors created a scalable pipeline that produces high-quality training data without relying on expensive reinforcement learning or closed recipes. Their resulting model, OpenThinker3-7B, achieved leading performance across major benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing open models and establishing new state-of-the-art results for small-scale reasoning architectures. This work enables developers to build competitive reasoning AI products using transparent, cost-effective methods, effectively democratizing access to advanced model training data and reducing reliance on walled-garden technology from major tech firms.

name: Lakshya A Agrawal
paper_title: GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning
synopsis: Current AI optimization techniques often require thousands of expensive model runs to improve performance, creating high computational costs. To solve this, the authors developed GEPA, a system that uses natural language reflection to diagnose errors and evolve prompts instead of relying on inefficient reinforcement learning rewards. Testing across multiple tasks shows GEPA outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 20% accuracy while using 35 times fewer resources. This approach enables efficient fine-tuning of complex AI agents for real-world applications where inference budgets or tool call costs are strictly limited.

name: Ion Stoica
paper_title: EditBench: Evaluating LLM Abilities to Perform Real-World Instructed Code Edits
synopsis: Existing benchmarks for AI coding assistants often use artificial problems that miss the complexities of real-world developer workflows where users provide natural instructions to modify highlighted code. To address this gap, the authors created EditBench, a dataset featuring 545 real-world problems collected from users that include critical context like cursor positions and highlighted code sections. Their analysis of 40 models reveals that performance varies drastically depending on contextual information, with only three models successfully solving more than 60% of these complex tasks. This research provides investors with a robust way to identify which AI coding tools truly understand context and user instructions, offering a clearer picture of their value beyond simple code generation.

name: Runyan Tan
paper_title: $p\textrm{-less}$ Sampling: A Robust Hyperparameter-Free Approach for LLM Decoding
synopsis: This paper addresses the fragility of current Large Language Model decoding methods, which often degrade in quality when temperature settings are increased to prioritize creativity or diversity. The authors introduce $p$-less sampling, a novel approach grounded in information theory that dynamically adjusts selection thresholds without requiring any hyperparameter tuning. Experimental results demonstrate that this method consistently outperforms existing strategies across math, reasoning, and creative writing tasks while reducing inference time and improving efficiency. This technique enables developers to deploy highly robust AI models that maintain high accuracy and output quality regardless of specific generation configurations or environment changes.

name: Jonathn Chang
paper_title: EigenBench: A Comparative Behavioral Measure of Value Alignment
synopsis: EigenBench addresses the critical challenge of quantifying subjective AI values like kindness or ethics without relying on human ground truth labels that are often unavailable or disputed. The method establishes a self-judging ecosystem where language models evaluate each other’s responses against specific value constitutions, using an EigenTrust algorithm to aggregate these peer judgments into a consensus score. Validation demonstrates that these model-derived metrics closely align with human evaluations and accurately rank models on benchmarks lacking objective answers. This framework enables practical use cases such as generating customized value leaderboards for organizations, objectively monitoring character training during fine-tuning, and identifying how different models interpret complex ethical principles.

paper_title: EigenBench: A Comparative Behavioral Measure of Value Alignment
synopsis: EigenBench addresses the critical challenge of quantifying subjective AI values like kindness or ethics without relying on human ground truth labels that are often unavailable or disputed. The method establishes a self-judging ecosystem where language models evaluate each other’s responses against specific value constitutions, using an EigenTrust algorithm to aggregate these peer judgments into a consensus score. Validation demonstrates that these model-derived metrics closely align with human evaluations and accurately rank models on benchmarks lacking objective answers. This framework enables practical use cases such as generating customized value leaderboards for organizations, objectively monitoring character training during fine-tuning, and identifying how different models interpret complex ethical principles.

name: Hideaki Kim
paper_title: A Representer Theorem for Hawkes Processes via Penalized Least Squares Minimization
synopsis: This paper addresses the computational bottleneck of estimating event interaction kernels in multivariate Hawkes processes, which are essential for modeling complex dynamics in finance and neuroscience. The authors derive a novel representer theorem using penalized least squares that analytically fixes dual coefficients to unity, removing the need for costly optimization typically required by existing methods. By leveraging random feature maps to solve integral equations efficiently, the proposed estimator reduces computational complexity to standard matrix operations independent of data size. This breakthrough enables highly scalable and accurate modeling of large-scale event sequences, unlocking practical deployment on massive datasets where traditional nonparametric approaches struggle.

name: Jinchuan Tian
paper_title: UALM: Unified Audio Language Model for Understanding, Generation and Reasoning
synopsis: This research addresses the inefficiency of current audio AI by introducing UALM, a unified model that combines audio understanding, generation, and reasoning into a single framework. The authors achieve state-of-the-art quality comparable to specialized diffusion models by utilizing specific training strategies such as data blending and classifier-free guidance within an auto-regressive language model architecture. A key breakthrough is the demonstration of cross-modal generative reasoning, allowing the system to use both text and audio tokens during intermediate thinking steps to solve complex tasks. This capability unlocks new applications in iterative creative workflows, such as AI assistants that can compose music, critique their own tracks, and refine compositions based on nuanced user instructions without needing separate specialized tools.

name: Yueqi Song
paper_title: Agent Data Protocol: Unifying Datasets for Diverse, Effective Fine-tuning of LLM Agents
synopsis: Large language model agents face a significant scaling bottleneck because high-quality training data is fragmented across incompatible formats and interfaces, preventing large-scale supervised fine-tuning. This paper introduces the Agent Data Protocol (ADP), a lightweight standardization layer that unifies thirteen existing datasets into a single representation for diverse tasks like coding and web browsing. Experiments confirm that fine-tuning with this unified protocol delivers approximately twenty percent average performance gains over base models while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art results. By lowering the engineering barrier to integrate heterogeneous data, ADP enables scalable, reproducible training pipelines that allow developers to leverage public resources efficiently across multiple agent frameworks.

name: Fan Yang
paper_title: LoongRL: Reinforcement Learning for Advanced Reasoning over Long Contexts
synopsis: LoongRL addresses the critical challenge of enabling large language models to reason deeply across extremely long documents, which is essential for complex tasks like legal analysis and debugging codebases but currently limited by scarce training data and high compute costs. The team introduces KeyChain, a method that transforms short questions into challenging long-context problems by inserting hidden clues among distracting text, forcing models to learn specific retrieval-and-reasoning patterns during reinforcement learning. Their results show that models trained on shorter 16K contexts can generalize to handle massive 128K tasks with significant accuracy improvements, matching performance from much larger and more expensive models. This breakthrough enables enterprise applications that need to analyze vast amounts of text for critical decisions without requiring prohibitive training infrastructure or sacrificing general reasoning skills.

name: Fan Yang
paper_title: TileLang: Bridge Programmability and Performance in Modern Neural Kernels
synopsis: Current AI kernel development faces a steep trade-off where high-level compilers lack the control needed for peak performance while manual coding is overly complex. This paper presents TILELANG, a tile-level programming system that grants explicit control over memory placement and parallelism while using automated inference to handle configuration details automatically. The authors demonstrate that their approach delivers up to 10x speedups on top GPUs compared to existing compilers like Triton while shrinking codebases by over 85% through hardware-aware defaults. This capability enables researchers to efficiently implement modern algorithms like FlashMLA and Multi-Head Attention, effectively bridging the gap between developer productivity and high-performance computing requirements.

name: Graham Neubig
paper_title: EditBench: Evaluating LLM Abilities to Perform Real-World Instructed Code Edits
synopsis: Existing benchmarks for AI coding assistants often use artificial problems that miss the complexities of real-world developer workflows where users provide natural instructions to modify highlighted code. To address this gap, the authors created EditBench, a dataset featuring 545 real-world problems collected from users that include critical context like cursor positions and highlighted code sections. Their analysis of 40 models reveals that performance varies drastically depending on contextual information, with only three models successfully solving more than 60% of these complex tasks. This research provides investors with a robust way to identify which AI coding tools truly understand context and user instructions, offering a clearer picture of their value beyond simple code generation.

name: Karen Ullrich
paper_title: OpenApps: Simulating Environment Variations to Measure UI Agent Reliability
synopsis: Current evaluations for autonomous AI assistants often rely on static app clones that fail to capture real-world variety, leading to overly optimistic reliability estimates. To address this, OpenApps introduces a lightweight framework capable of generating thousands of variable versions of common apps, allowing researchers to evaluate agents under diverse design and content conditions without expensive hardware. The study reveals that agent performance can fluctuate by more than half depending on these environmental changes, proving that standard testing methods significantly overestimate an agent's trustworthiness. This tool enables companies to stress-test autonomous agents thoroughly before deployment, ensuring they remain robust when navigating the unpredictable nature of real user environments.

paper_title: OpenApps: Simulating Environment Variations to Measure UI Agent Reliability
synopsis: Current evaluations for autonomous AI assistants often rely on static app clones that fail to capture real-world variety, leading to overly optimistic reliability estimates. To address this, OpenApps introduces a lightweight framework capable of generating thousands of variable versions of common apps, allowing researchers to evaluate agents under diverse design and content conditions without expensive hardware. The study reveals that agent performance can fluctuate by more than half depending on these environmental changes, proving that standard testing methods significantly overestimate an agent's trustworthiness. This tool enables companies to stress-test autonomous agents thoroughly before deployment, ensuring they remain robust when navigating the unpredictable nature of real user environments.

name: Brandon Livio Annesi
paper_title: Overparametrization bends the landscape: BBP transitions at initialization in simple Neural Networks
synopsis: This research investigates how increasing the number of parameters in simple neural networks alters the mathematical shape of their training loss landscape, specifically affecting when optimization algorithms can successfully recover hidden signals. Through theoretical analysis of the loss curvature at initialization, the authors demonstrate that wider models shift critical phase transitions to require lower signal quality for successful learning compared to narrower networks. Practically, these findings enable more robust spectral initialization methods for tasks like signal reconstruction and explain why overparameterized networks often generalize better in high-dimensional applications such as optics or quantum mechanics.

name: Hongjian Jiang
paper_title: Softmax Transformers are Turing-Complete
synopsis: This paper addresses whether standard Chain-of-Thought transformers using soft attention can theoretically solve any computational problem, resolving a long-standing theoretical gap regarding model trainability and scaling. The authors demonstrate Turing-completeness by extending a counting-based framework with relative positional encodings to allow simulation of arbitrary languages rather than restricted inputs. Their main finding confirms that these models remain trainable and scalable across complex arithmetic reasoning tasks without relying on unrealistic hard attention mechanisms. This breakthrough validates the reliability of large language models for long-horizon logical problem solving, offering assurance to investors regarding the fundamental capabilities of current transformer architectures to handle complex mathematical reasoning.

name: Raghu Arghal
institution: University of Pennsylvania, University of Pennsylvania
paper_title: Steering the Herd: A Framework for LLM-based Control of Social Learning
synopsis: This paper addresses how Large Language Models and algorithms shape user decisions by strategically controlling information flow within a social learning environment where users learn from one another. The authors introduce a new mathematical framework that models optimal strategies for planners aiming either to maximize user welfare or to induce specific actions while accounting for these sequential social dynamics. Their simulations demonstrate that these algorithmic mediators can intentionally obscure information to steer public opinion, significantly impacting social welfare even under strict transparency constraints. For investors and regulators, this work provides a tractable basis for understanding the risks and ethical dynamics of AI-driven recommendation systems deployed at scale in industries like search and news.

name: Zachary Izzo
paper_title: Quantitative Bounds for Length Generalization in Transformers
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of enabling transformers to handle input sequences significantly longer than those seen during training. The authors provide the first quantitative bounds on the minimum training data required for this "length generalization" to occur across various model settings including finite and infinite precision. They demonstrate that generalization is achievable when the internal behavior on long sequences can be simulated by shorter ones, with necessary data scaling based on model complexity and vocabulary size. For industry stakeholders, these findings clarify how to efficiently scale context lengths in large language models, optimizing compute resources needed for robust next-generation AI applications.

name: Zhiheng Xi
paper_title: AgentGym-RL: An Open-Source Framework to Train LLM Agents for Long-Horizon Decision Making via Multi-Turn RL
synopsis: This paper addresses the critical lack of unified open-source frameworks for training Large Language Model agents on complex, long-horizon decision-making tasks using reinforcement learning. The researchers introduce AgentGym-RL, a modular system that supports diverse environments through a staged training approach called Scaling Inter-RL, which progressively expands interaction to prevent instability and encourage deeper exploration. Experiments show that this method allows smaller open-source models to match or exceed commercial benchmarks like OpenAI o3 and Gemini across 27 different real-world scenarios. Ultimately, this work enables developers to build cost-effective, high-performance autonomous agents for applications ranging from web navigation and deep search to scientific tasks without relying solely on expensive internal reasoning capabilities.

name: Jiazheng Zhang
paper_title: FRABench and UFEval: Unified Fine-grained Evaluation with Task and Aspect Generalization
synopsis: This paper addresses the bottleneck in evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models where existing tools struggle to generalize across different tasks and specific quality criteria. To solve this, the researchers developed FRABench, a comprehensive dataset of over 60,000 samples spanning 112 aspects, and UFEval, a unified evaluator that learns to assess diverse visual tasks simultaneously. Their findings show that joint learning across these domains creates synergistic effects, allowing the system to generalize better to unseen criteria than specialized models. These innovations provide venture capitalists with a robust tool for consistently benchmarking multimodal AI capabilities and aligning model behavior with human preferences through direct preference optimization.

Experiments show that this method allows smaller open-source models to match or exceed commercial benchmarks like OpenAI o3 and Gemini across 27 different real-world scenarios. Ultimately, this work enables developers to build cost-effective, high-performance autonomous agents for applications ranging from web navigation and deep search to scientific tasks without relying solely on expensive internal reasoning capabilities.

name: Thibaud Gloaguen
paper_title: LLM Fingerprinting via Semantically Conditioned Watermarks
synopsis: Current LLM fingerprinting methods are too brittle to survive common deployment changes like finetuning or quantization, making it difficult for owners to enforce licenses on open-weight models. To solve this, researchers introduce a robust paradigm using broad semantic domains and statistical watermark signals embedded directly into the generated text rather than relying on memorized specific keys. The study demonstrates that this fingerprint is stealthy and maintains 100% detection rates across various adversarial scenarios and model modifications without impacting utility. This technology allows companies to reliably verify ownership and track usage of distributed AI models, ensuring compliance and protecting intellectual property even when the underlying model weights are altered.

name: Nikita Maksimovich Kornilov
paper_title: Universal Inverse Distillation for Matching Models with Real-Data Supervision (No GANs)
synopsis: This paper addresses the slow inference speeds of modern generative models like diffusion and flow matching by proposing a new distillation framework that naturally utilizes real data without complex adversarial training. The authors introduce RealUID, which unifies previous methods under a single universal loss function, eliminating the need for extra discriminator networks typically required in GAN-based approaches. By streamlining the distillation process with simple yet rigorous theoretical explanations, the method achieves efficient one-step generation while avoiding the computational overhead associated with adversarial losses. Consequently, this research unlocks faster and more cost-effective deployment of high-fidelity AI models across diverse architectures, addressing a major barrier to scaling generative applications in production environments.

name: Houyi Li
paper_title: Mixture-of-Experts Can Surpass Dense LLMs Under Strictly Equal Resource
synopsis: This research investigates whether Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures can outperform traditional dense LLMs when constrained by identical budgets for parameters, compute, and data—a common limitation in current industry comparisons. The authors developed a novel optimization framework that tunes MoE activation rates and employs a data reuse strategy to eliminate hidden resource disparities often overlooked in past studies. Their extensive experiments demonstrate that properly designed MoE models achieve superior performance on both training and downstream tasks compared to dense counterparts under these strict equal-resource conditions. Practically, this finding validates the economic efficiency of MoE designs, allowing venture-backed AI companies to deploy more powerful models without increasing infrastructure or data collection costs.

name: Xiangyu Zhang
paper_title: NextStep-1: Toward Autoregressive Image Generation with Continuous Tokens at Scale
synopsis: Current high-fidelity image generation relies on computationally heavy diffusion models or suffers from quality loss in discrete autoregressive approaches. This paper introduces NextStep-1, a 14-billion parameter model that overcomes these limits by using continuous tokens within a stable autoregressive framework paired with a lightweight flow matching head. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance in text-to-image generation and editing while maintaining the efficiency and scalability of standard language models. These advancements enable developers to build practical tools for high-quality image synthesis and editing without the excessive computational costs associated with traditional diffusion architectures.

name: Jatan Shrestha
paper_title: Pareto-Conditioned Diffusion Models for Offline Multi-Objective Optimization
synopsis: This research tackles offline multi-objective optimization, where finding the best balance between competing goals like cost and performance is difficult because evaluating new designs is too expensive or risky. The authors introduce Pareto-Conditioned Diffusion (PCD), a new model that directly generates solutions based on target trade-offs without relying on unreliable surrogate predictors. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach achieves competitive performance with significantly greater consistency across diverse tasks compared to existing methods. Consequently, PCD enables more efficient and reliable design optimization for critical fields such as biological sequence engineering and complex architecture search where testing is costly.

name: Maksim Kolodiazhnyi
institution: Moscow State University, Lomonosov Moscow State University
paper_title: cadrille: Multi-modal CAD Reconstruction with Reinforcement Learning
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of accurately reconstructing 3D engineering models from diverse inputs like images, text, and point clouds simultaneously, where current multimodal methods typically fail to match single-input quality. The authors introduce cadrille, a model that utilizes reinforcement learning fine-tuning alongside supervised training to generate executable Python code for CAD reconstruction. Results indicate this system sets new state-of-the-art performance across ten benchmarks while handling real-world data better than existing approaches. This advancement significantly lowers the barrier for entry into design workflows, enabling users to create precise, editable 3D models directly from smartphone photos or text descriptions, which is highly valuable for accelerating prototyping in manufacturing and engineering industries.

name: Fan Chen
paper_title: The Coverage Principle: How Pre-Training Enables Post-Training
synopsis: This paper addresses the fundamental gap between standard pre-training losses and downstream model performance by introducing "coverage," a metric that specifically measures how much probability mass a model assigns to high-quality responses. The researchers prove that next-token prediction naturally optimizes this coverage profile, allowing models to generalize faster and perform better on scaling methods like Best-of-N sampling compared to relying solely on cross-entropy loss. These theoretical insights lead to practical improvements such as using tournament procedures for checkpoint selection and gradient normalization to boost model quality before fine-tuning begins. Consequently, companies can more efficiently select high-performing pre-trained models and implement reliable test-time scaling strategies without incurring unnecessary computational costs.

name: Noah Golowich
paper_title: Sequences of Logits Reveal the Low Rank Structure of Language Models
synopsis: This paper investigates the internal mathematical structure of Large Language Models by treating them as sequential probabilistic maps with low-dimensional representations. The authors empirically demonstrate that extended logit matrices across various prompts possess a consistent low-rank property, allowing the model's information to be approximated using significantly fewer dimensions. Consequently, this finding enables generating coherent responses to specific targets by combining outputs from unrelated or even nonsensical prompts without accessing them directly. Ultimately, this research unlocks new pathways for evaluating AI safety risks and developing theoretical guarantees for how these models process and generate language information.

These theoretical insights lead to practical improvements such as using tournament procedures for checkpoint selection and gradient normalization to boost model quality before fine-tuning begins. Consequently, companies can more efficiently select high-performing pre-trained models and implement reliable test-time scaling strategies without incurring unnecessary computational costs.

name: Yang Hu
paper_title: Conformal Robustness Control: A New Strategy for Robust Decision
synopsis: Current robust decision-making tools often sacrifice efficiency for safety by enforcing strict coverage constraints that lead to overly conservative outcomes and higher potential losses. This paper introduces Conformal Robustness Control, a new framework that directly optimizes prediction sets under explicit robustness requirements rather than relying on less efficient coverage guarantees. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach significantly lowers worst-case costs compared to existing methods while still meeting required safety standards. Consequently, this technique unlocks more efficient strategies for high-stakes industries like portfolio management, medical diagnosis, and transportation planning where minimizing failures is critical without limiting effectiveness.

name: Mohamed Malhou
paper_title: HATSolver: Learning Gröbner Bases with Hierarchical Attention Transformers
synopsis: This paper addresses the scalability bottleneck in using AI to solve complex systems of non-linear polynomial equations, which are fundamental to cryptography and optimization but often too computationally expensive for standard models. The authors introduce Hierarchical Attention Transformers that utilize a tree-structured architecture to process data hierarchically, significantly reducing memory costs compared to traditional flat attention mechanisms. Their method successfully computes these bases for systems with up to 13 variables at full density, surpassing previous limits of five variables and competing directly with established algebraic tools. This breakthrough enables more efficient security verification for cryptographic standards like AES and faster computational solutions in computer vision and biology.

name: Florian Fervers
paper_title: It's All Just Vectorization: einx, a Universal Notation for Tensor Operations
synopsis: Modern tensor programming relies on complex NumPy-like notation that is prone to shape errors and lacks consistency across different operations. This paper introduces `einx`, a universal notation for tensor operations that unifies these inconsistent rules into a single, declarative system based on vectorization concepts. The research demonstrates how this approach reduces diverse library functions into a minimal set of elementary operations while integrating seamlessly with existing frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow. Ultimately, this innovation simplifies code maintenance and reduces debugging overhead for developers building scalable AI systems across multiple computational environments.

name: Chinmay Datar
paper_title: Fast training of accurate physics-informed neural networks without gradient descent
synopsis: Current Physics-Informed Neural Networks struggle to solve complex physical equations efficiently because they rely on slow optimization algorithms that often ignore the natural flow of time. This research introduces Frozen-PINN, which sidesteps these issues by freezing spatial components and using standard time-evolution solvers instead of traditional gradient descent training. Testing on nine different benchmarks reveals the new method is up to five orders of magnitude faster while matching or exceeding the accuracy of existing state-of-the-art models. This breakthrough unlocks rapid simulation capabilities for dynamical systems in physics, biology, and engineering, offering a scalable tool that does not require specialized hardware or extensive tuning.

name: Guhao Feng
paper_title: In-Place Test-Time Training
synopsis: Large Language Models typically struggle with evolving tasks because they are static after training and cannot update their knowledge during inference. This paper introduces In-Place Test-Time Training, which overcomes architectural barriers by updating specific parameters within existing model blocks using efficient chunk-wise processing aligned with next-token prediction goals. Experiments show this method allows a 4B-parameter model to handle contexts up to 128k tokens while outperforming competitive approaches without costly retraining from scratch. Consequently, companies can deploy models that continuously adapt to streaming data and maintain high performance over long-term evolving tasks in real-world environments.

name: Shengjie Luo
paper_title: Quotient-Space Diffusion Models
synopsis: This paper addresses inefficiencies in diffusion models used for scientific tasks, such as molecular generation, where systems waste computational resources learning unnecessary symmetrical movements like rotation. The authors introduce a quotient-space framework that mathematically treats equivalent structures as identical units, allowing the model to ignore redundant data while still generating accurate results. Their approach significantly simplifies training by removing the need to learn specific symmetry changes while guaranteeing the sampling process recovers the correct target distribution. Tested on small molecules and proteins, this method outperforms previous state-of-the-art models, offering more efficient and high-quality tools for AI-driven drug discovery and molecular design.

name: Eran Malach
paper_title: To Infinity and Beyond: Tool-Use Unlocks Length Generalization in State Space Models
synopsis: State Space Models (SSMs) offer lower inference costs than Transformers but historically struggle with tasks requiring long-context reasoning due to their fixed memory constraints. This paper demonstrates that granting SSMs interactive access to external tools acts as a form of unbounded memory, enabling them to overcome inherent limitations and achieve length generalization on arbitrarily complex problems. Theoretical analysis and experiments confirm that tool-augmented SSMs can solve advanced arithmetic, coding, and reasoning tasks at scales far exceeding their training data size. Ultimately, this approach unlocks efficient, scalable alternatives for building autonomous agents in agentic settings like software engineering where cost-effective long-form generation is critical.

paper_title: To Infinity and Beyond: Tool-Use Unlocks Length Generalization in State Space Models
synopsis: State Space Models (SSMs) offer lower inference costs than Transformers but historically struggle with tasks requiring long-context reasoning due to their fixed memory constraints. This paper demonstrates that granting SSMs interactive access to external tools acts as a form of unbounded memory, enabling them to overcome inherent limitations and achieve length generalization on arbitrarily complex problems. Theoretical analysis and experiments confirm that tool-augmented SSMs can solve advanced arithmetic, coding, and reasoning tasks at scales far exceeding their training data size. Ultimately, this approach unlocks efficient, scalable alternatives for building autonomous agents in agentic settings like software engineering where cost-effective long-form generation is critical.

name: Lei Wang
paper_title: TileLang: Bridge Programmability and Performance in Modern Neural Kernels
synopsis: Current AI kernel development faces a steep trade-off where high-level compilers lack the control needed for peak performance while manual coding is overly complex. This paper presents TILELANG, a tile-level programming system that grants explicit control over memory placement and parallelism while using automated inference to handle configuration details automatically. The authors demonstrate that their approach delivers up to 10x speedups on top GPUs compared to existing compilers like Triton while shrinking codebases by over 85% through hardware-aware defaults. This capability enables researchers to efficiently implement modern algorithms like FlashMLA and Multi-Head Attention, effectively bridging the gap between developer productivity and high-performance computing requirements.

name: Marco Fumero
institution: Institute of Science and Technology Austria(ISTA)
paper_title: Navigating the Latent Space Dynamics of Neural Models
synopsis: This paper reframes autoencoder neural networks as dynamical systems that naturally define a latent vector field through standard training processes without additional tuning. The authors demonstrate that regularization techniques create attractor points within this space, which mathematically encode the model's memorization and generalization capabilities directly in its weights. By analyzing these latent trajectories, developers can extract embedded prior knowledge without input data and effectively detect out-of-distribution samples or distribution shifts during deployment. Consequently, this research offers a practical framework for auditing foundation models to ensure robustness and safety before scaling up to real-world scenarios.

name: Riccardo Poiani
paper_title: Non-Asymptotic Analysis of (Sticky) Track-and-Stop
synopsis: This study addresses the theoretical gap in pure exploration problems by providing non-asymptotic guarantees for the Track-and-Stop (TAS) and Sticky Track-and-Stop algorithms, which were previously only proven optimal as error rates approached zero. The authors derive precise performance bounds that validate these methods remain highly efficient even with realistic, finite confidence levels without needing algorithmic changes. A key finding is that this rigorous analysis applies to the Sticky version for the first time, covering scenarios where multiple correct answers exist, such as selecting near-optimal options. These results enable more reliable deployment of bandit algorithms in practical sectors like clinical trials and digital advertising, ensuring robust decision-making when managing risk constraints during early data collection.

name: Aideen Fay
paper_title: The Shape of Adversarial Influence: Characterizing LLM Latent Spaces with Persistent Homology
synopsis: This study addresses the critical gap in Large Language Model security by investigating how adversarial attacks reshape the complex, nonlinear geometry of internal representations that standard methods ignore. The authors utilize Persistent Homology to map these latent spaces and discover a consistent topological signature where malicious inputs cause the model’s activation space to compress into fewer, larger-scale structures compared to normal diverse inputs. This finding demonstrates that the technique is robust across different model architectures and provides an interpretable geometric explanation for why certain attacks succeed. By quantifying these shape changes, the framework enables the development of advanced detection systems capable of identifying subtle adversarial shifts that are invisible to traditional linear classifiers.

name: Harry Amad
paper_title: Hyperparameter Trajectory Inference with Conditional Lagrangian Optimal Transport
synopsis: This paper addresses the expensive problem of retraining neural networks when user preferences change after deployment by enabling behavioral adaptation at inference time. The authors propose Hyperparameter Trajectory Inference, a technique that uses Conditional Lagrangian Optimal Transport to learn how model outputs shift across different hyperparameter settings without full retraining. By applying physics-inspired mathematical principles like least-action paths, the method accurately reconstructs neural behavior from limited data points while handling complex dynamics. Testing confirms this approach outperforms existing alternatives, unlocking applications such as personalized reinforcement learning for medical treatment and flexible uncertainty quantification in regression tasks.

name: Haijier Chen
paper_title: Vid-LLM: A Compact Video-based 3D Multimodal LLM with Reconstruction–Reasoning Synergy
synopsis: Vid-LLM addresses the high cost and scalability limitations of existing 3D vision models that depend on complex external data inputs like point clouds or rigid registration modules. The researchers propose a compact framework using only standard video feeds to jointly reconstruct scene geometry and perform multimodal reasoning through a specialized Cross-Task Adapter. Extensive experiments confirm the model achieves superior performance in tasks like 3D question answering and visual grounding without requiring expensive preprocessing pipelines. This approach enables scalable, real-world deployment for robotics and embodied AI systems that need deep spatial understanding from simple camera streams.

name: Marco Bondaschi
paper_title: From Markov to Laplace: How Mamba In-Context Learns Markov Chains
synopsis: This paper addresses the theoretical gap surrounding Mamba, a faster alternative to transformers currently used in large-scale language modeling. The authors investigate how Mamba learns from context using Markov chains and reveal that its convolution mechanism enables optimal statistical estimation comparable to sophisticated estimators like Laplacian smoothing. Key findings show that even a single-layer version of the model achieves performance limits previously thought to require deeper architectures, validating the efficiency of state space models. These insights unlock scalable paths for building next-generation AI systems that maintain high accuracy while reducing the computational costs and energy consumption typical of traditional transformer-based designs.

name: Caglar Gulcehre
paper_title: Partition Generative Modeling: Masked Modeling Without Masks
synopsis: Current masked generative models waste significant computational power by processing empty mask tokens during generation, leading to slow sampling speeds. This paper introduces Partition Generative Models that group tokens together instead of masking them, enabling fast parallel decoding while focusing only on relevant information. Experiments show this new approach achieves five times faster text generation and seven times faster image creation compared to existing models while maintaining high-quality outputs. This efficiency boost allows developers to deploy cost-effective AI systems capable of real-time content creation and low-latency inference for applications like instant video synthesis or rapid text summarization.
