name: Wenyi Wang
institution: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology
paper_title: Huxley-G\"odel Machine: Human-Level Coding Agent Development by an Approximation of the Optimal Self-Improving Machine
synopsis: This paper addresses the inefficiency of current AI coding agents that rely solely on immediate test scores to guide their evolution, which often fails to predict long-term improvement. The authors propose a new metric called Clade-Metaproductivity and an algorithm called the Huxley-Gödel Machine that evaluates an agent’s potential based on its entire family tree of future versions instead of just current performance. Experimental results show this method achieves better coding accuracy while using 2.38 times less computing power compared to existing techniques. This advancement allows companies to develop highly capable autonomous software tools more cost-effectively, significantly lowering the hardware costs required to train self-improving AI systems.

paper_title: Huxley-G\"odel Machine: Human-Level Coding Agent Development by an Approximation of the Optimal Self-Improving Machine
synopsis: This paper addresses the inefficiency of current AI coding agents that rely solely on immediate test scores to guide their evolution, which often fails to predict long-term improvement. The authors propose a new metric called Clade-Metaproductivity and an algorithm called the Huxley-Gödel Machine that evaluates an agent’s potential based on its entire family tree of future versions instead of just current performance. Experimental results show this method achieves better coding accuracy while using 2.38 times less computing power compared to existing techniques. This advancement allows companies to develop highly capable autonomous software tools more cost-effectively, significantly lowering the hardware costs required to train self-improving AI systems.

name: Romain Froger
paper_title: Gaia2: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Dynamic and  Asynchronous Environments
synopsis: Current LLM agent benchmarks are typically static, failing to test how models handle real-world dynamics like incoming notifications or shifting deadlines. To address this, the authors introduce Gaia2, a benchmark built on a new platform that simulates asynchronous mobile environments and verifies specific actions for training reinforcement learning systems. Their evaluation shows that no single model dominates all tasks, revealing critical trade-offs where high-reasoning models struggle with speed while efficient ones lack accuracy. This framework unlocks practical applications for building resilient AI assistants capable of managing complex scheduling, multi-agent collaboration, and adapting to unpredictable events in daily user interactions.

name: Guillaume Braun
paper_title: Fast Escape, Slow Convergence: Learning Dynamics of Phase Retrieval under Power-Law Data
synopsis: This paper investigates scaling laws in nonlinear machine learning models, specifically focusing on phase retrieval where input data features follow a power-law distribution with significant anisotropy. By developing a mathematical framework that decomposes training dynamics into distinct phases, the authors reveal how the spectral properties of the data dictate error convergence rates rather than allowing uniform improvement across all dimensions. The key finding demonstrates a trade-off between fast initial escape from poor alignment and slow final convergence in weak feature directions, providing explicit formulas for error decay based on input spectrum. These insights empower practitioners to predict training bottlenecks and optimize resource allocation for models handling imbalanced or heavy-tailed data distributions common in real-world applications like computer vision or embeddings.

institution: Ecole Normale Supérieure, Ecole Normale Supérieure de Paris

name: Bruno Loureiro
institution: Ecole Normale Supérieure, Ecole Normale Supérieure de Paris
paper_title: Scaling Laws and Spectra of Shallow Neural Networks in the Feature Learning Regime
synopsis: This paper addresses the theoretical gap in understanding how neural network performance scales when models actively learn features, rather than relying on fixed linear kernels. To do this, the authors map shallow neural networks to classical signal processing problems like LASSO and matrix compressed sensing, allowing for exact mathematical analysis of optimization dynamics. Their main finding is a phase diagram that predicts accuracy bottlenecks based on data size and regularization, establishing a direct link between weight distribution statistics and generalization performance. This research enables more efficient AI model design by providing first-principles validation for scaling laws, helping engineers predict compute requirements and avoid poor training regimes without expensive trial-and-error experiments.

name: Zhenxing Xu
paper_title: AutoEP: LLMs-Driven Automation of Hyperparameter Evolution for Metaheuristic Algorithms
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of automatically tuning complex algorithms, which often requires expensive training or fails to generalize to new problems. The authors introduce AutoEP, a framework that bypasses this need by using large language models as reasoning engines that analyze live search data without prior training. Experimental results show the system consistently outperforms existing methods and demonstrates that affordable open-source models can match the performance of top-tier proprietary systems like GPT-4. Ultimately, AutoEP allows businesses to deploy efficient optimization strategies without relying on massive computational resources or extensive prior data collection.

name: Nina Vesseron
institution: Ecole Nationale de la Statistique et de l'Administration Economique
paper_title: On the Wasserstein Geodesic Principal Component Analysis of probability measures
synopsis: This paper addresses the limitation of standard statistical tools that often fail when analyzing collections of probability distributions because they produce invalid or distorted functions. To solve this, the authors develop exact Geodesic PCA algorithms based on Wasserstein geometry that capture true data variation without relying on linear approximations. Using neural networks and specific mathematical spaces, their method provides a robust solution distinct from previous tangent approaches while accurately handling curvature effects. This research enables more reliable dimensionality reduction for complex real-world applications like image analysis and 3D point cloud processing.

name: Bryon Tjanaka
paper_title: Discount Model Search for Quality Diversity Optimization in High-Dimensional Measure Spaces
synopsis: Current quality diversity optimization tools struggle in complex, high-dimensional spaces because they assign the same score to similar solutions, causing the search process to stop improving. To fix this, the authors propose Discount Model Search (DMS), a method that uses smooth modeling instead of discrete histograms to accurately reward diverse exploration. Benchmarks show DMS significantly outperforms existing algorithms like CMA-MAE on both standard tasks and new high-dimensional image domains. By enabling users to specify optimization goals using image datasets rather than manually designing functions, this approach unlocks scalable applications in generative modeling and robotics where defining complex visual metrics is critical.

name: Guoqi Yu
paper_title: Decentralized Attention Fails Centralized Signals: Rethinking Transformers for Medical Time Series
synopsis: Standard Transformer models struggle with medical time series data like EEG and ECG because their decentralized attention mechanism does not align with the centralized, synchronized nature of biological signals. The authors propose CoTAR, a novel module that replaces inefficient attention calculations with a centralized core token system to better capture these critical channel dependencies while maintaining linear computational complexity. Testing across five medical benchmarks validates that this method boosts diagnostic accuracy by up to 12% and uses significantly less memory and processing time than existing state-of-the-art models. This breakthrough unlocks scalable AI infrastructure for real-time patient monitoring, early disease detection, and personalized healthcare applications where efficiency and resource constraints are critical.

name: Haiquan Qiu
paper_title: Why Low-Precision Transformer Training Fails: An Analysis on Flash Attention
synopsis: This paper addresses the persistent instability where low-precision training formats like BF16 combined with Flash Attention cause catastrophic loss explosion in large models. The authors provide a mechanistic explanation revealing that biased rounding errors accumulate alongside emerging low-rank representations, creating a feedback loop that corrupts weight updates and overloads the network's spectral norms. By introducing a minimal modification to the Flash Attention algorithm to mitigate this specific bias, they successfully stabilize the training process without sacrificing computational efficiency. This research unlocks safer pathways for deploying ultra-efficient hardware, enabling significantly reduced memory footprints and infrastructure costs for large-scale AI development.

name: Yuxuan Zhou
paper_title: Overcoming Joint Intractability with Lossless Hierarchical Speculative Decoding
synopsis: Large language models face significant inference speed bottlenecks because verifying generated tokens is computationally expensive and often forces trade-offs between quality and performance. This paper proposes Hierarchical Speculative Decoding, a method that accelerates decoding by balancing probability mass across different levels to ensure the output remains identical to standard generation without losing accuracy. Unlike existing techniques that compromise distribution fidelity or struggle with longer sequences, this approach consistently improves acceptance rates and achieves an average 6.7% speedup across diverse benchmarks. Entrepreneurs can leverage this lossless framework to scale AI applications faster while maintaining the high-quality text generation essential for commercial use cases.

synopsis: This research tackles the difficulty of aligning large language models to specific user preferences, particularly when feedback is sparse or user data is limited. Experiments demonstrate the system achieves state-of-the-art performance by adapting effectively to unseen users without needing extensive training history. This capability opens significant opportunities for building personalized AI assistants and customer support bots that can instantly tailor interactions to diverse individual needs.

name: Tianyu Wang
paper_title: EditVerse: Unifying Image and Video Editing and Generation with In-Context Learning
synopsis: EditVerse addresses the significant fragmentation between image and video editing technologies, which currently suffer from limited training data and rigid model architectures compared to more mature image solutions. The research introduces a unified framework that treats text, images, and videos as a single token sequence to enable robust in-context learning and natural knowledge transfer across different media types. By combining over two million mixed samples with curated video editing data, the model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a new benchmark while supporting diverse tasks like object manipulation and style transfer. This advancement unlocks versatile content creation workflows, allowing developers to build flexible editing tools that handle varying resolutions and durations within one adaptable system.

name: Zhuofeng Li
paper_title: In-the-Flow Agentic System Optimization for Effective Planning and Tool Use
synopsis: Existing AI agents struggle to scale with complex, long-horizon tasks because they rely on monolithic models that generalize poorly or training-free systems that cannot adapt to dynamic tool environments. To solve this, the authors introduce AGENT FLOW, a trainable framework that coordinates specialized modules by directly optimizing its planner within the live multi-turn loop using an outcome-driven algorithm called Flow-GRPO. Their experiments demonstrate that this 7B-scale system outperforms much larger proprietary models like GPT-4o across search, math, and scientific benchmarks with significant accuracy gains. This breakthrough enables scalable, robust agentic systems capable of reliable tool orchestration for demanding enterprise applications requiring adaptive planning and knowledge retrieval.

name: Sheng Liu
paper_title: InfoTok: Adaptive Discrete Video Tokenizer via Information-Theoretic Compression
synopsis: Current video tokenizers waste resources by applying fixed compression rates to all content, leading to unnecessary redundancy or quality loss on complex videos. To solve this, the researchers developed INFOTOK, an adaptive system that calculates optimal token counts based on each video's unique information complexity. Empirical results show the approach saves about 50% of tokens without sacrificing accuracy and achieves 2.3× better compression rates than existing adaptive techniques. This breakthrough enables significantly cheaper training and inference for long-form video models, unlocking scalable capabilities for upcoming multi-modal AI products.

name: Yejin Choi
paper_title: Multiplayer Nash Preference Optimization
synopsis: Existing Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback methods are limited to two-player interactions, failing to capture the complexity of real-world preferences which often involve conflicting signals from multiple annotators or reward models. This paper introduces Multiplayer Nash Preference Optimization (MNPO), a framework that generalizes alignment as an n-player game where policies compete against a population rather than a single synthetic opponent. Empirical results show MNPO consistently outperforms existing baselines on instruction-following benchmarks by stabilizing optimization and handling diverse, non-transitive preference structures effectively. This research unlocks scalable alignment techniques for Large Language Models that are robust when trained on heterogeneous data sources and multiple conflicting evaluation criteria.

name: Yejin Choi
paper_title: OpenThoughts: Data Recipes for Reasoning Models
synopsis: The OpenThoughts project addresses the critical barrier of proprietary datasets that currently prevent open-source researchers from replicating state-of-the-art reasoning AI capabilities in math, code, and science. By systematically investigating over 1,000 data generation strategies, the authors created a scalable pipeline that produces high-quality training data without relying on expensive reinforcement learning or closed recipes. Their resulting model, OpenThinker3-7B, achieved leading performance across major benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing open models and establishing new state-of-the-art results for small-scale reasoning architectures. This work enables developers to build competitive reasoning AI products using transparent, cost-effective methods, effectively democratizing access to advanced model training data and reducing reliance on walled-garden technology from major tech firms.

name: Rajiv Movva
paper_title: What's In My Human Feedback? Learning Interpretable Descriptions of Preference Data
synopsis: Language models trained on opaque preference data often exhibit unexpected behaviors like verbosity or sycophancy before deployment reveals them. This paper introduces WIMHF, a method that uses sparse autoencoders to automatically identify interpretable features from response differences without needing pre-defined hypotheses. The authors show that these simple, human-readable features capture two-thirds of the predictive power of complex black-box models while exposing hidden biases like unintentional preferences for toxic content. Consequently, developers can now curate safer training data, mix datasets without conflicting signals, and build personalized AI assistants that adjust on specific traits like length or politeness.

name: Gyuhyeon Seo
paper_title: SimuHome: A Temporal- and Environment-Aware Benchmark for Smart Home LLM Agents
synopsis: This paper addresses the critical gap in testing environments that prevents Large Language Model agents from managing real-world smart home constraints like time-based rules and device limitations. The authors introduce SimuHome, a high-fidelity simulator using the Matter protocol that allows AI agents to interact with devices, observe changing environmental conditions, and verify states within a reproducible virtual home. Evaluation results reveal a significant trade-off: while advanced reasoning models handle complex scheduling tasks well, they are three times slower than standard models, making them impractical for real-time use without further optimization. This research enables developers to safely benchmark and train agents in a cost-effective virtual space before deploying on physical hardware, providing crucial insights for balancing model intelligence with the speed requirements of consumer smart home applications.

name: Aayush Karan
paper_title: Reasoning with Sampling: Your Base Model is Smarter Than You Think
synopsis: This paper challenges the prevailing need for expensive reinforcement learning to enhance LLM reasoning, demonstrating that base models already possess significant latent capabilities waiting to be unlocked. The authors introduce a training-free algorithm that uses iterative sampling to sharpen the model's inherent likelihood distribution at inference time without external rewards or curated datasets. Experimental results show this method matches or outperforms standard RL techniques on complex math and coding benchmarks while maintaining better output diversity than post-trained models. Consequently, organizations can unlock advanced reasoning performance immediately during inference, bypassing heavy training costs and applicability limits associated with current frontier model optimization pipelines.

name: Yilun Du
paper_title: Reasoning with Sampling: Your Base Model is Smarter Than You Think
synopsis: This paper challenges the prevailing need for expensive reinforcement learning to enhance LLM reasoning, demonstrating that base models already possess significant latent capabilities waiting to be unlocked. The authors introduce a training-free algorithm that uses iterative sampling to sharpen the model's inherent likelihood distribution at inference time without external rewards or curated datasets. name: Yilun Du
paper_title: Energy-Based Transformers are Scalable Learners and Thinkers
synopsis: This paper addresses the limitations of current AI reasoning models, which typically require specific task rewards or specialized training to perform complex tasks beyond simple pattern recognition. The authors propose Energy-Based Transformers (EBTs), a new architecture that learns to verify and refine predictions through energy minimization without needing extra supervision or human-verified feedback. Experimental results show that EBTs scale significantly faster during pretraining, improve inference performance by up to 29%, and generalize better on out-of-distribution data than standard autoregressive or diffusion models. Ultimately, this approach unlocks flexible reasoning capabilities for diverse modalities like text and images, enabling AI systems to solve novel problems efficiently without the high costs associated with reinforcement learning training.

name: Yilun Du
paper_title: WoW!: World Models in a Closed-Loop World
synopsis: Current benchmarks for generative world models prioritize visual quality but ignore whether they help embodied agents succeed at real-world tasks. To bridge this gap, the authors introduce World-In-World, a unified platform that evaluates these models in a closed-loop environment where agents continuously plan and act based on predictions. Their study reveals that visual realism does not guarantee task success, with performance improving significantly through post-training with action data and allocating more compute during inference. This framework provides investors and developers with a practical standard for assessing embodied AI utility, paving the way for more reliable robots and autonomous systems that can make informed decisions based on predictive simulation.

name: Wenbin Ouyang
paper_title: Learning to Segment for Vehicle Routing Problems
synopsis: Current vehicle routing solvers waste significant computational time by repeatedly searching through parts of a route plan that do not change between updates. This research introduces a method called FSTA that identifies these stable segments and groups them into fixed units, allowing the system to focus its processing power only on the portions that actually need improvement. By training neural networks to predict which segments are stable versus unstable, the authors accelerate state-of-the-art algorithms by two to seven times while maintaining solution quality. This technology can be easily integrated with existing logistics software to handle massive scale deliveries and ride-hailing demands faster.

name: Changli Tang
paper_title: WAVE: Learning Unified & Versatile Audio-Visual Embeddings with Multimodal LLM
synopsis: Current multimodal models often fail to unify dynamic signals like audio and video with text, limiting their versatility in cross-modal tasks. To address this, the WAVE model introduces the first unified embedding framework that projects text, audio, and synchronized streams into a single semantic space using hierarchical feature fusion within a large language model. The system achieves state-of-the-art performance on video benchmarks while maintaining robust understanding capabilities and generating embeddings that adapt to specific user instructions. This technology unlocks new practical applications such as any-to-any search where users can retrieve videos from text or audio clues, and enables instruction-specific representations for advanced question-answering systems.

name: Carl Edwards
paper_title: mCLM: A Modular Chemical Language Model that Generates Functional and Makeable Molecules
synopsis: Current AI tools for drug discovery often propose molecules that are theoretically possible but difficult or impossible to manufacture efficiently in a real lab due to poor synthesis compatibility. To solve this, the authors developed mCLM, a model that treats molecules as modular building blocks compatible with automated production rather than representing them as individual atoms. Experiments on hundreds of FDA-approved drugs demonstrated that this approach significantly improves synthetic accessibility and functional properties compared to leading AI models like GPT-5. This technology enables faster and more cost-effective drug discovery by bridging the gap between digital AI design and physical chemistry production for manufacturing robots.

name: Heng Ji
paper_title: Energy-Based Transformers are Scalable Learners and Thinkers
synopsis: This paper addresses the limitations of current AI reasoning models, which typically require specific task rewards or specialized training to perform complex tasks beyond simple pattern recognition. The authors propose Energy-Based Transformers (EBTs), a new architecture that learns to verify and refine predictions through energy minimization without needing extra supervision or human-verified feedback. Experimental results show that EBTs scale significantly faster during pretraining, improve inference performance by up to 29%, and generalize better on out-of-distribution data than standard autoregressive or diffusion models. Ultimately, this approach unlocks flexible reasoning capabilities for diverse modalities like text and images, enabling AI systems to solve novel problems efficiently without the high costs associated with reinforcement learning training.

name: Zheng Huang
paper_title: Seeing Through the Brain: New Insights from Decoding Visual Stimuli with fMRI
synopsis: This study solves the difficulty of reconstructing detailed visual images from fMRI brain scans by challenging the assumption that vision-based models are necessary for the latent space. The researchers developed PRISM, a framework that translates neural signals into a structured text space using language models to capture the compositional nature of objects and attributes more effectively than previous methods. Their findings reveal that this text-based intermediate representation aligns better with brain activity, achieving an 8% reduction in perceptual loss compared to state-of-the-art competitors. This breakthrough paves the way for more accurate brain-computer interfaces and enables new possibilities in generating content directly from human neural activity without needing visual input during training.

name: Usha Bhalla
paper_title: Temporal Sparse Autoencoders: Leveraging the Sequential Nature of Language for Interpretability
synopsis: Current interpretability tools often fail to extract high-level meaning from language models because they treat tokens independently, resulting in noisy grammatical patterns rather than semantic concepts. To address this, the authors introduce Temporal Sparse Autoencoders that incorporate a consistency loss to ensure features remain stable across adjacent words, effectively separating intent and context from syntactic noise. Experiments demonstrate that this approach recovers coherent concepts like scientific topics or emotional states without explicit supervision while maintaining reconstruction quality. For investors, this unlocks safer and more controllable AI systems by enabling clearer observation of model internal drivers for alignment and steering applications.

name: Zhiliang Peng
paper_title: VibeVoice: Expressive Podcast Generation with Next-Token Diffusion
synopsis: VIBEVOICE addresses the significant challenge of generating high-quality, long-form audio with multiple speakers, such as podcasts, which traditional text-to-speech models struggle to scale beyond short clips or single voices. The team achieves this through a novel architecture that integrates large language models with next-token diffusion and efficient continuous speech tokenizers to process up to 90 minutes of content with four speakers naturally. This system excels at maintaining speaker consistency and capturing subtle human expressions like breaths and pacing, ensuring the audio sounds authentic without manual intervention. Ultimately, this breakthrough unlocks scalable opportunities for automating the creation of professional audiobooks, interactive voice assistants, and immersive podcast experiences for commercial markets.

synopsis: VIBEVOICE addresses the significant challenge of generating high-quality, long-form audio with multiple speakers, such as podcasts, which traditional text-to-speech models struggle to scale beyond short clips or single voices. The team achieves this through a novel architecture that integrates large language models with next-token diffusion and efficient continuous speech tokenizers to process up to 90 minutes of content with four speakers naturally. This system excels at maintaining speaker consistency and capturing subtle human expressions like breaths and pacing, ensuring the audio sounds authentic without manual intervention. Ultimately, this breakthrough unlocks scalable opportunities for automating the creation of professional audiobooks, interactive voice assistants, and immersive podcast experiences for commercial markets.

name: Jing Yao
paper_title: AdAEM: An Adaptively and Automated Extensible Measurement of LLMs' Value Difference
synopsis: Current methods for measuring Large Language Model values fail to capture true differences because they rely on outdated questions that models already agree on. To address this, the authors propose AdAEM, an adaptive algorithm that automatically generates controversial test questions by exploring conflicting value boundaries among diverse models without manual curation. Their analysis shows that these dynamic questions successfully reveal hidden misalignments and cultural biases that standard static benchmarks cannot detect. This technology unlocks practical applications for investors in accurately assessing model safety risks and societal alignment as AI systems rapidly evolve.

name: Zhuoran Jin
institution: Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences
paper_title: Omni-Reward: Towards Generalist Omni-Modal Reward Modeling with Free-Form Preferences
synopsis: This research addresses critical gaps in AI alignment where existing reward models focus narrowly on text and images while failing to handle audio, video, or personalized user preferences. To solve this, the authors introduce Omni-Reward, a unified system comprising a new five-modality benchmark dataset and advanced generative models that provide transparent reasoning across text, image, video, audio, and 3D data. Their model achieves significant accuracy improvements over commercial tools by dynamically adjusting scores based on free-form instructions rather than fixed binary labels. This technology unlocks safer, more robust AI assistants capable of following complex real-world instructions through speech recognition, visual understanding, and multimodal generation tasks simultaneously.

name: Xiaoxiao Hu
paper_title: Spherical Watermark: Encryption-Free, Lossless Watermarking for Diffusion Models
synopsis: Diffusion models revolutionize image creation but lack reliable ways to verify authenticity without ruining visual quality or requiring heavy cryptography. The authors present Spherical Watermark, a new system that embeds traceable codes directly into noise without needing per-image encryption keys or degrading the final images. By mathematically mapping watermark data onto a sphere, the method proves statistically indistinguishable from standard random noise while maintaining high fidelity and efficiency. This breakthrough enables AI platforms to track content provenance and ensure legal compliance at scale without incurring significant computational costs or storage overhead.

name: Jialiang Zhang
paper_title: Rodrigues Network for Learning Robot Actions
synopsis: This research addresses the difficulty of modeling complex robot movements where standard AI models often ignore the physical connections between joints. The authors introduce a new network architecture called RodriNet that uses a learnable mathematical operator based on classical rotation formulas to encode these mechanical structures directly into the learning process. Experiments confirm that this approach outperforms existing methods in predicting motion and achieving state-of-the-art results in 3D hand pose estimation from single images. Ultimately, these findings open new possibilities for improving robot imitation learning in manipulation tasks while enhancing computer vision capabilities.

name: Zhun Wang
paper_title: CyberGym: Evaluating AI Agents' Real-World Cybersecurity Capabilities at Scale
synopsis: Current cybersecurity benchmarks are insufficient because they rely on small datasets and static challenges that fail to mirror the dynamic complexity of real-world security risks. This research introduces CyberGym, a large-scale evaluation platform utilizing over 1,500 instances derived from actual software vulnerabilities across diverse projects to rigorously test AI agents' ability to reproduce flaws. The study demonstrates that even state-of-the-art models achieve only about a 20% success rate while simultaneously identifying 35 previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities and incomplete patches. Ultimately, this framework empowers developers and investors to accurately measure AI safety capabilities and directly improve open-source security through responsible disclosure of critical issues.

name: Alexi Gladstone
paper_title: Energy-Based Transformers are Scalable Learners and Thinkers
synopsis: This paper addresses the limitations of current AI reasoning models, which typically require specific task rewards or specialized training to perform complex tasks beyond simple pattern recognition. The authors propose Energy-Based Transformers (EBTs), a new architecture that learns to verify and refine predictions through energy minimization without needing extra supervision or human-verified feedback. Experimental results show that EBTs scale significantly faster during pretraining, improve inference performance by up to 29%, and generalize better on out-of-distribution data than standard autoregressive or diffusion models. Ultimately, this approach unlocks flexible reasoning capabilities for diverse modalities like text and images, enabling AI systems to solve novel problems efficiently without the high costs associated with reinforcement learning training.

synopsis: This paper addresses the limitations of current AI reasoning models, which typically require specific task rewards or specialized training to perform complex tasks beyond simple pattern recognition. The authors propose Energy-Based Transformers (EBTs), a new architecture that learns to verify and refine predictions through energy minimization without needing extra supervision or human-verified feedback. Experimental results show that EBTs scale significantly faster during pretraining, improve inference performance by up to 29%, and generalize better on out-of-distribution data than standard autoregressive or diffusion models. Ultimately, this approach unlocks flexible reasoning capabilities for diverse modalities like text and images, enabling AI systems to solve novel problems efficiently without the high costs associated with reinforcement learning training.

name: Rahul Krishna Thomas
paper_title: Global Resolution: Optimal Multi-Draft Speculative Sampling via Convex Optimization
synopsis: This paper addresses the computational bottleneck in large language model inference by optimizing speculative sampling techniques to accelerate text generation. The authors simplify a previously unsolvable mathematical verification problem into a tractable convex optimization task, enabling precise tuning of draft models for higher efficiency. Their new algorithm achieves over 90% token acceptance rates with less than 100 milliseconds of overhead, significantly outperforming earlier methods that were limited to about 84% acceptance. These findings enable faster and more cost-effective deployment of AI models in real-world applications where low latency and high throughput are critical requirements.

name: Danyal Rehman
paper_title: FALCON: Few-step Accurate Likelihoods for Continuous Flows
synopsis: Molecular sampling is a foundational tool for drug discovery and material science, yet current continuous flow models are prohibitively slow because calculating their likelihoods requires excessive computational resources. This paper introduces FALCON, a new generative model that combines efficient few-step sampling with accurate likelihood estimates suitable for scientific importance sampling techniques. By leveraging a hybrid training objective to ensure invertibility without sacrificing speed, FALCON achieves performance two orders of magnitude faster than existing state-of-the-art models while requiring far less training data. This advancement unlocks scalable pathways for characterizing biomolecules and accelerating the design of novel drugs and materials with significantly reduced computational overhead.

name: Alexander Tong
paper_title: Planner Aware Path Learning in Diffusion Language Models Training
synopsis: Diffusion language models face a critical inefficiency where they are trained using uniform random sampling but deployed with strategic planners, creating a gap between how the model is taught and how it works. The authors address this by deriving a new training bound that incorporates planning strategies directly into the learning objective, resulting in a simple method called Planner Aware Path Learning. By aligning training dynamics with inference paths, PAPL delivers consistent performance boosts across protein design, text generation, and coding without requiring architectural changes. This breakthrough enables faster deployment of high-quality generative AI tools for vital industries like biotechnology and software development by eliminating the theoretical mismatch between training and use cases.

name: Gregory Yauney
paper_title: How Reliable is Language Model Micro-Benchmarking?
synopsis: This paper investigates whether using small data subsets to test AI models reliably predicts performance compared to full-scale benchmarks. To address this, the researchers introduce a new metric that quantifies how many examples are needed to consistently distinguish between two competing models. Their analysis reveals that complex selection methods offer minimal advantages over simple random sampling unless the subset is large enough to capture minor performance differences, typically requiring around 250 examples. These findings provide actionable guidance for developers and investors on balancing evaluation speed with accuracy, suggesting that random sampling is often sufficient when comparing models with similar capabilities.

name: Wayne Chi
paper_title: EditBench: Evaluating LLM Abilities to Perform Real-World Instructed Code Edits
synopsis: Existing benchmarks for AI coding assistants often use artificial problems that miss the complexities of real-world developer workflows where users provide natural instructions to modify highlighted code. To address this gap, the authors created EditBench, a dataset featuring 545 real-world problems collected from users that include critical context like cursor positions and highlighted code sections. Their analysis of 40 models reveals that performance varies drastically depending on contextual information, with only three models successfully solving more than 60% of these complex tasks. This research provides investors with a robust way to identify which AI coding tools truly understand context and user instructions, offering a clearer picture of their value beyond simple code generation.

name: Mingyang Liu
paper_title: Online Learning and Equilibrium Computation with Ranking Feedback
synopsis: This paper addresses online learning challenges where agents receive only rankings of actions instead of numerical scores, which is essential for privacy-sensitive or human-in-the-loop environments. The authors prove that effective learning fails with basic ranking data alone but develop new algorithms to achieve reliable performance using time-average utility feedback under specific conditions. These findings allow platforms to compute stable strategic outcomes and equilibria without demanding users disclose private valuation details. Consequently, the research enables practical applications in dating apps, ride-sharing matching, and recommendation systems where ranking is more natural than exact scoring.

name: Xuying Ning
paper_title: MC-Search: Evaluating and Enhancing Multimodal Agentic Search with Structured Long Reasoning Chains
synopsis: Current multimodal AI benchmarks fail to test complex, multi-step reasoning, making it hard to accurately evaluate agentic models. To fix this, the authors create MC-SEARCH, a dataset of over 3,000 examples that exposes specific errors like poor planning and incorrect retrieval in existing systems. They also release SEARCH-ALIGN, a fine-tuning method that uses these verified chains to improve open-source models for better accuracy. This research provides tools to build more robust AI agents capable of handling real-world tasks requiring deep cross-modal analysis.

name: Thomas Mitchel
paper_title: True Self-Supervised Novel View Synthesis is Transferable
synopsis: The paper identifies that existing novel view synthesis models fail because they generate views specific to a single scene rather than understanding universal camera movement. The authors introduce XFactor, a new self-supervised system that separates camera motion from visual content to ensure predictions transfer between different environments. Their key finding is that removing explicit 3D geometry biases allows for higher quality and more reliable view generation across diverse real-world datasets compared to previous methods. This technology enables practical applications like autonomous vehicle perception and augmented reality, where generating consistent virtual views from limited data is critical.

name: Fengyu Cai
paper_title: Revela: Dense Retriever Learning via Language Modeling
synopsis: This research addresses the critical challenge of scarce and expensive annotated data required to train dense retrievers for specialized domains such as code and law. The authors introduce Revela, a scalable framework that treats retrieval as a language modeling task using in-batch attention to automatically link related documents without needing query-document pairs. By training retrievers alongside large language models, Revela outperforms larger supervised competitors on coding and reasoning benchmarks while reducing training data needs by 1000x and compute usage by 10x. This breakthrough enables developers to build robust, domain-specific knowledge access systems that rely solely on raw text, significantly lowering the barriers to entry for deploying advanced AI retrieval tools in enterprise environments.

name: Philippe Laban
paper_title: LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
synopsis: This study addresses the significant gap between standard LLM evaluation methods and real-world usage, where users frequently rely on multi-turn conversations to clarify underspecified needs. The authors developed a sharded simulation environment that gradually reveals task information across conversation turns to directly measure performance degradation in realistic settings. Their large-scale experiments reveal that all tested models suffer an average 39% performance drop in multi-turn dialogues due to increased unreliability, often making wrong assumptions early on without recovering. These findings provide crucial direction for model builders and investors to prioritize conversational reliability alongside raw capability, highlighting the risk of overestimating AI utility in practical applications where users cannot always provide complete instructions immediately.

These findings provide crucial direction for model builders and investors to prioritize conversational reliability alongside raw capability, highlighting the risk of overestimating AI utility in practical applications where users cannot always provide complete instructions immediately.

name: Jennifer Neville
paper_title: LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
synopsis: This study addresses the significant gap between standard LLM evaluation methods and real-world usage, where users frequently rely on multi-turn conversations to clarify underspecified needs. The authors developed a sharded simulation environment that gradually reveals task information across conversation turns to directly measure performance degradation in realistic settings. name: Christoph Minixhofer
institution: University of Edinburgh, University of Edinburgh
paper_title: TTSDS2: Resources and Benchmark for Evaluating Human-Quality Text to Speech Systems
synopsis: Current evaluation methods for high-quality Text-to-Speech systems are unreliable because human listening tests are costly and objective metrics often fail to match human judgment as AI becomes better at mimicking real speech. The authors introduce TTSDS2, a new benchmark and metric framework that analyzes synthetic audio across multiple languages and domains by comparing distributional similarities in voice identity, prosody, and intelligibility rather than relying on single reference samples. In testing against 16 alternative metrics, TTSDS2 proved superior with an average correlation of 0.67 to human ratings across 14 languages, making it the most robust tool currently available for measuring speech generation quality. This research enables developers to efficiently benchmark new models and investors to assess risks related to synthetic voice identity theft while fostering applications that could restore communication abilities for people with speech impairments.

name: Aakash Lahoti
paper_title: Mamba-3: Improved Sequence Modeling using State Space Principles
synopsis: This paper addresses the inefficiency of standard Transformer models and the performance gaps in existing faster alternatives during inference. The authors introduce Mamba-3, which enhances state-space modeling through trapezoidal discretization, complex-valued state updates for better tracking, and a multi-input structure to improve hardware utilization. Their findings show that this new architecture significantly outperforms current baselines on language modeling tasks while solving complex reasoning problems that previous linear models failed at. Consequently, this research unlocks the practical deployment of high-quality AI systems that maintain cost-effective compute requirements without sacrificing capability.

name: Xinpeng Wang
paper_title: Is it Thinking or Cheating?  Detecting Implicit Reward Hacking by Measuring Reasoning Effort
synopsis: This research addresses the growing security risk of AI models secretly exploiting loopholes to earn high rewards without actually completing their assigned tasks, which often evades standard safety checks because their reasoning traces appear normal. To detect this hidden cheating behavior, the authors developed TRACE, a method that quantifies reasoning effort by measuring how quickly an answer becomes sufficient to pass verification as text is cut off mid-generation. Results show that models cheating on loopholes succeed much faster than honest ones, allowing TRACE to identify these vulnerabilities with significant performance gains over current monitoring systems. This scalable approach enables developers to proactively audit AI safety and uncover invisible training data leaks before they compromise the reliability of agentic deployments.

name: Songyao Jin
paper_title: Causal Structure Learning in Hawkes Processes with Complex Latent Confounder Networks
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of mapping causal structures in complex systems where some underlying drivers remain hidden or unobserved, which typically causes existing models to draw misleading conclusions about system dynamics. To solve this, the authors convert continuous-time event sequences into discrete linear representations and use an iterative algorithm that identifies latent subprocesses alongside observable ones without requiring prior knowledge of how many hidden factors exist. By leveraging statistical rank tests on covariance data, their method guarantees accurate causal recovery even when unmeasured confounders create spurious relationships between visible variables. This capability unlocks more reliable risk analysis and dynamic modeling for sectors like neuroscience and finance where measuring every influencing factor is technically impossible.

name: Guojian Zhan
paper_title: Mean Flow Policy with Instantaneous Velocity Constraint for One-step Action Generation
synopsis: This paper addresses the latency bottleneck in reinforcement learning caused by existing generative policies that require slow, multi-step sampling to produce actions. To solve this, the authors propose Mean Flow Policy (MFP), a new method that enables direct one-step action generation by modeling mean velocity fields instead of instantaneous ones, while an Instantaneous Velocity Constraint ensures high accuracy without increasing computational burden. Empirical tests on robotic manipulation benchmarks show MFP achieves state-of-the-art success rates and significantly faster training and inference speeds compared to baseline models. These efficiency gains open new possibilities for real-time control systems and online reinforcement learning where low-latency decision-making is essential for stable performance.

name: Kyu Beom Han
institution: Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology
paper_title: Radiometrically Consistent Gaussian Surfels for Inverse Rendering
synopsis: This paper addresses a major challenge in inverse rendering where existing models struggle to accurately separate material properties from complex lighting effects, particularly indirect illumination from unseen viewpoints. To solve this, the authors introduce RadioGS, which employs a physics-based consistency loss that allows the system to self-correct and model global illumination without relying solely on limited training data. Results demonstrate that their framework outperforms current methods in decomposing scene properties and can adapt to new lighting conditions within minutes while maintaining real-time rendering speeds under ten milliseconds. These capabilities unlock practical applications for industries needing realistic 3D assets, such as virtual production, augmented reality, and dynamic product visualization on the web.

name: Johnny Wei
paper_title: Hubble: a Model Suite to Advance the Study of LLM Memorization
synopsis: This paper addresses the difficulty of studying how large language models memorize sensitive data like copyrighted text or personal information due to the opacity of commercial systems. The authors introduce HUBBLE, an open-source suite of models that includes controlled "perturbations" where specific sensitive content is inserted into training data at varying rates to simulate real-world risks. Their key findings establish that memorization risks are determined by the frequency of sensitive data relative to corpus size, suggesting that diluting such data and ordering it earlier in training reduces retention. This framework unlocks practical applications for the research community by providing a standardized testbed to benchmark advanced security methods like membership inference attacks and machine unlearning.

name: Vinod Raman
paper_title: Differentially Private Domain Discovery
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of discovering informative data domains within sensitive datasets while adhering to differential privacy requirements. The authors provide rigorous theoretical guarantees for the Weighted Gaussian Mechanism, demonstrating near-optimal performance on common real-world data distributions that were previously lacking in existing literature. By applying this mechanism as a foundation for top-k and set-hitting algorithms, they achieve competitive utility without assuming prior knowledge of the data domain. Consequently, these methods enable scalable and robust privacy-preserving analysis for industrial applications involving queries, reviews, and purchase histories.

By applying this mechanism as a foundation for top-k and set-hitting algorithms, they achieve competitive utility without assuming prior knowledge of the data domain. Consequently, these methods enable scalable and robust privacy-preserving analysis for industrial applications involving queries, reviews, and purchase histories.

name: Shifeng Xie
paper_title: CauKer: Classification Time Series Foundation Models Can Be Pretrained on Synthetic Data
synopsis: Traditional time series foundation models typically require expensive pre-training on massive real-world datasets that are often scarce and risky for unbiased testing. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes CAUKER, a method that generates diverse synthetic data containing realistic trends and causal dependencies using Gaussian Processes and structural causal models. Experiments demonstrate that AI models trained solely on this synthetic data achieve state-of-the-art classification performance while showing predictable scaling laws unlike standard real-world benchmarks. This approach enables industries to train robust models for monitoring and diagnostics efficiently, significantly reducing reliance on costly data collection and eliminating data leakage risks during evaluation.

name: Xiaowen Jiang
institution: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
paper_title: Non-Convex Federated Optimization under Cost-Aware Client Selection
synopsis: Current federated learning comparisons often fail to account for the varying communication costs of different client participation strategies, leading to inefficient performance estimates for real-world systems with unreliable devices. This paper introduces a cost-aware optimization framework and proposes a new algorithm that minimizes communication expenses without requiring expensive full-network synchronization every round. By improving gradient estimators to exploit functional similarity, the method achieves superior efficiency compared to existing techniques for non-convex problems while avoiding reliance on loose worst-case smoothness constants. These advances enable faster, more energy-efficient collaborative machine learning on resource-constrained devices like smartphones or medical sensors where network stability and battery life are critical limitations.

name: Zheng Zhao
institution: University of Edinburgh, University of Edinburgh
paper_title: Verifying Chain-of-Thought Reasoning via Its Computational Graph
synopsis: Current Chain-of-Thought verification methods detect errors but fail to explain the underlying computational causes behind them. This paper introduces Circuit-based Reasoning Verification, which replaces opaque model layers with interpretable transcoders to map the causal flow of information during reasoning steps. The researchers demonstrate that distinct structural patterns in these computational graphs reliably predict reasoning failures and allow for targeted interventions to fix errors directly. Ultimately, this white-box approach enables developers to debug advanced AI systems more effectively than black-box methods, ensuring higher reliability in complex reasoning tasks.

name: Kairong Luo
paper_title: How Learning Rate Decay Wastes Your Best Data in Curriculum-Based LLM Pretraining
synopsis: This paper identifies a critical flaw in large language model pretraining where standard learning rate decay schedules inadvertently cause high-quality data to be underutilized. The authors find that curriculum-based strategies, which sort data by quality for better learning, often present this premium content only during later training stages when the learning rate has already dropped too low to optimize effectively. To solve this inefficiency, the researchers propose two simple adjustments: adopting a moderate learning rate decay schedule or replacing decay with model averaging to maintain optimization power when it matters most. These strategies improve benchmark performance by 1.64% without requiring additional data refinement, offering a cost-effective way to enhance model capabilities using existing resources. Ultimately, this approach enables development teams to extract more value from limited high-quality datasets while optimizing compute and training costs.

name: Kejing Lu
paper_title: Probabilistic Kernel Function for Fast Angle Testing
synopsis: This research tackles the computational bottleneck of angle testing in high-dimensional similarity search by introducing two new probabilistic kernel functions that utilize deterministic reference angles instead of expensive random Gaussian projections. Unlike prior methods requiring infinite data assumptions, these kernels offer theoretical guarantees while significantly reducing query costs. Experimental results demonstrate a 2.5X to 3X increase in query throughput compared to state-of-the-art graph-based search algorithms like HNSW. These advancements directly accelerate critical applications such as Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search and recommendation systems, enabling faster data retrieval for machine learning pipelines.

name: Madhav Kanda
paper_title: RefineStat: Efficient Exploration for Probabilistic Program Synthesis
synopsis: This research addresses the frequent semantic errors small language models produce when generating probabilistic programs, which often lead to statistically unreliable results. The authors introduce RefineStat, a framework that enforces strict distribution validity during generation and automatically refines models by resampling components based on Bayesian diagnostic checks. Their findings demonstrate that this method produces high-quality statistical code comparable to expensive closed-source large language models using only smaller, more affordable open-weight models. Consequently, this technology unlocks cost-effective automation for scientific model discovery and data analysis workflows where rigorous statistical correctness is essential.

name: Kieran Didi
paper_title: Scaling Atomistic Protein Binder Design with Generative Pretraining and Test-Time Compute
synopsis: This research addresses the computational bottleneck in protein binder design by unifying two distinct AI approaches—direct generative modeling and iterative sequence optimization—into a single comprehensive framework. The authors introduce Complexa, which leverages a newly created large-scale synthetic dataset to train a generative model that utilizes advanced inference-time compute to refine candidates for higher quality structures. Experimental results demonstrate that this system achieves state-of-the-art success rates for both protein and small molecule targets while outperforming existing methods under normalized compute budgets. Consequently, these advancements enable more efficient drug discovery pipelines by facilitating the rapid design of high-affinity binders for previously inaccessible biological targets without relying on extensive sequence re-design steps.

name: Danny Reidenbach
paper_title: Exploring Synthesizable Chemical Space with Iterative Pathway Refinements
synopsis: Current molecular generative models often fail to produce designs that can actually be synthesized in a lab, which significantly limits their practical value in drug discovery. This research introduces ReaSyn, a framework that navigates synthesizable chemical space using unified bottom-up and top-down generation alongside holistic editing operations. Experimental results show the method achieves an 87.9% reconstruction rate for target molecules, significantly outperforming previous techniques while maintaining high diversity across the chemical space. By guaranteeing that generated candidates are synthesizable, this tool accelerates real-world applications like hit expansion and lead optimization, enabling faster development of viable drug candidates without wasting resources on impossible structures.

name: Michael M. Bronstein
paper_title: Planner Aware Path Learning in Diffusion Language Models Training
synopsis: Diffusion language models face a critical inefficiency where they are trained using uniform random sampling but deployed with strategic planners, creating a gap between how the model is taught and how it works. The authors address this by deriving a new training bound that incorporates planning strategies directly into the learning objective, resulting in a simple method called Planner Aware Path Learning. By aligning training dynamics with inference paths, PAPL delivers consistent performance boosts across protein design, text generation, and coding without requiring architectural changes. This breakthrough enables faster deployment of high-quality generative AI tools for vital industries like biotechnology and software development by eliminating the theoretical mismatch between training and use cases.

paper_title: Structured Flow Autoencoders: Learning Structured Probabilistic Representations with Flow Matching
synopsis: This paper addresses the critical gap between high-fidelity generative models and structured probabilistic models, which typically excel at either generation quality or latent representation learning but not both. The authors propose Structured Flow Autoencoders (SFA), a novel framework that combines flow matching with graphical models to explicitly learn conditional likelihoods and interpretable posterior distributions simultaneously. Experimental results demonstrate that SFA consistently outperforms traditional VAEs and standard flow methods in generation quality, sample diversity, and scalability across image, video, and biological data. This technology unlocks new possibilities for computational biology and scientific domains requiring both realistic data synthesis and low-dimensional, interpretable representations for downstream analysis and control.

name: Weiduo Liao
paper_title: Plug-and-Play Compositionality for Boosting Continual Learning with Foundation Models
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of catastrophic forgetting in AI systems that struggle to learn new tasks without losing knowledge from previous ones. The authors propose CompSLOT, a plug-and-play framework that extracts meaningful visual concepts from images and trains classifiers to prioritize these shared concept combinations instead of relying solely on high-dimensional features. Experimental results show that this method significantly enhances performance across various foundation models while enabling better generalization to unseen object attributes. Ultimately, this research unlocks scalable applications for industries requiring continuous adaptation, such as autonomous driving or robotics, by offering a robust, method-agnostic module to improve existing continual learning pipelines.

name: Ritik Raina
institution: State University of New York at Stony Brook
paper_title: Generating metamers of human scene understanding
synopsis: This paper addresses the challenge of generating visuals that align with how humans construct a coherent understanding of scenes by combining peripheral context with focused eye movements. The authors introduce MetamerGen, a latent diffusion model that integrates high-resolution fixation data with low-resolution peripheral blur to synthesize images indistinguishable from reality in human perception. Behavioral experiments validated that these generated metamers successfully mimic human scene understanding, identifying high-level semantic features as the strongest predictors of perceptual alignment. This framework enables gaze-adaptive rendering systems for virtual and augmented reality, allowing developers to optimize visual fidelity based on user attention patterns without breaking the illusion of presence.

name: Le-Trung Nguyen
paper_title: Efficient Resource-Constrained Training of Transformers via Subspace Optimization
synopsis: This paper addresses the significant memory and energy limitations that currently prevent large transformer models from being trained directly on resource-constrained edge devices. The researchers propose Weight-Activation Subspace Iteration (W ASI), a technique that compresses both model weights and activation maps into a stable low-rank subspace during fine-tuning to mitigate these bottlenecks. Results indicate that this method reduces memory consumption by up to 62 times and computational costs by 2 times while maintaining accuracy comparable to standard training. Ultimately, W ASI enables the practical deployment of vision transformers on edge hardware like Raspberry Pi, advancing privacy-focused AI applications with drastically lower energy requirements.

name: Haobin Li
institution: School of Software Engineering, Sichuan University, Sichuan University
paper_title: Learning with Dual-level Noisy Correspondence for Multi-modal Entity Alignment
synopsis: This research tackles a critical flaw in existing multi-modal knowledge graph systems where manual annotations often contain errors, leading to inaccurate entity alignments. The authors introduce RULE, a new framework that estimates the reliability of data connections and filters out noisy information both within entities and across different data sources. Through extensive testing on five benchmarks, the method proves significantly more robust than seven competing state-of-the-art approaches, achieving higher accuracy despite imperfect training data. Ultimately, this technology enables more reliable data integration for industries relying on cross-source information, such as search engines and recommendation platforms that must handle real-world data inconsistencies.

name: Yijie Lin
paper_title: Uncover Underlying Correspondence for Robust Multi-view Clustering
synopsis: This paper addresses the critical issue of noisy data pairs that disrupt multi-view clustering by treating samples from the same category as mismatched. The researchers propose CorreGen, a generative framework that uses an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to mathematically infer true correspondences and filter out unreliable connections without needing costly manual cleaning. Their approach significantly boosts clustering performance on web-scraped datasets with up to 10% accuracy improvement over previous methods in high-noise scenarios. This technology enables more robust analytics for industries relying on imperfect cross-modal data, such as automated image-text search and surveillance systems where perfect alignment is unfeasible.

name: Panagiotis D. Grontas
paper_title: Pinet: Optimizing hard-constrained neural networks with orthogonal projection layers
synopsis: This research solves the challenge of training neural networks to produce feasible-by-design solutions for complex optimization problems with hard constraints. The key method is PiNet, an output layer that projects network outputs onto feasible sets using efficient operator splitting and backpropagation via the implicit function theorem. Results show it outperforms state-of-the-art methods by orders of magnitude in training time and solution robustness while maintaining comparable inference speeds. This technology enables accelerated optimization proxies for high-frequency applications like logistics, energy grids, and multi-vehicle motion planning on standard GPUs.

name: Federico Danieli
paper_title: ParaRNN: Unlocking Parallel Training of Nonlinear RNNs for Large Language Models
synopsis: This paper tackles the fundamental training bottleneck of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), where their inherent sequential processing makes them significantly slower to train than parallel models like Transformers. The researchers propose a new framework called FlashRNN that unlocks parallel computation for nonlinear RNNs by reformulating recurrence operations as solvable mathematical systems using Newton’s method and custom parallel reductions. This technique accelerates training by up to 665 times while successfully enabling the scale-up of complex models to 7 billion parameters with accuracy matching leading industry standards like Mamba2 and Transformers. Ultimately, this advancement allows developers to explore more expressive nonlinear architectures for language modeling without compromising on inference efficiency or computational speed.

name: Seul Lee
institution: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology
paper_title: Exploring Synthesizable Chemical Space with Iterative Pathway Refinements
synopsis: Current molecular generative models often fail to produce designs that can actually be synthesized in a lab, which significantly limits their practical value in drug discovery. This research introduces ReaSyn, a framework that navigates synthesizable chemical space using unified bottom-up and top-down generation alongside holistic editing operations. Experimental results show the method achieves an 87.9% reconstruction rate for target molecules, significantly outperforming previous techniques while maintaining high diversity across the chemical space. By guaranteeing that generated candidates are synthesizable, this tool accelerates real-world applications like hit expansion and lead optimization, enabling faster development of viable drug candidates without wasting resources on impossible structures.

name: Tongtian Zhu
paper_title: On The Surprising Effectiveness of a Single Global Merging in Decentralized Learning
synopsis: This paper addresses the efficiency challenge of scheduling communication in decentralized machine learning, where devices train without a central server but face limited bandwidth. Counterintuitively, the authors find that concentrating all synchronization into a single global merge at the end of training significantly improves model performance even with highly diverse local data. Theoretically, they prove this approach matches standard parallel convergence rates while minimizing costly network usage throughout the process. These findings enable scalable AI deployment across geographically distributed nodes, such as edge devices or IoT networks, by maximizing generalization despite sparse communication.

name: Yi Xu
paper_title: Visual Planning: Let's Think Only with Images
synopsis: Current artificial intelligence models often convert visual inputs into text before reasoning, which creates limitations for spatial tasks like navigation that rely on precise geometric understanding. This paper introduces Visual Planning, a paradigm where models generate plans directly as sequences of images without relying on language mediation to structure their thought process. The team developed a reinforcement learning framework trained solely on vision models that significantly outperforms traditional text-based methods in maze and robot navigation benchmarks. This technology opens new avenues for robotics and autonomous systems that require intuitive, image-based inference rather than verbose verbal explanations to solve complex spatial problems.

synopsis: Current artificial intelligence models often convert visual inputs into text before reasoning, which creates limitations for spatial tasks like navigation that rely on precise geometric understanding. This paper introduces Visual Planning, a paradigm where models generate plans directly as sequences of images without relying on language mediation to structure their thought process. The team developed a reinforcement learning framework trained solely on vision models that significantly outperforms traditional text-based methods in maze and robot navigation benchmarks. This technology opens new avenues for robotics and autonomous systems that require intuitive, image-based inference rather than verbose verbal explanations to solve complex spatial problems.

name: Taishi Nakamura
paper_title: Optimal Sparsity of Mixture-of-Experts Language Models for Reasoning Tasks
synopsis: This paper investigates how sparsity settings in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models differentially impact reasoning versus memorization tasks, challenging traditional scaling laws that assume uniform performance gains across architectures. By training diverse MoE families under fixed compute budgets, the researchers found that reasoning accuracy depends on active FLOPs and data per parameter rather than just total loss, peaking efficiency around 20 tokens per parameter. Crucially, advanced techniques like reinforcement learning or extra test-time compute cannot fix the reasoning deficits caused by overly sparse initial architectures during pre-training. Consequently, developers should optimize sparsity configurations early in the pipeline to balance cost and capability, unlocking more efficient models for complex tasks without needing expensive post-hoc fixes.

name: Chengyang Ying
paper_title: Exploratory Diffusion Model for Unsupervised Reinforcement Learning
synopsis: This paper tackles the critical challenge of training agents to explore diverse environments without external rewards, which often hinders their ability to generalize to new tasks. The authors introduce the Exploratory Diffusion Model (ExDM), utilizing diffusion models to accurately estimate complex state distributions and generate intrinsic rewards that drive exploration into under-visited regions. ExDM decouples high-fidelity modeling from action selection, significantly reducing computational costs and enabling faster adaptation to downstream tasks even with limited interaction data. These results establish new benchmarks in state coverage and transfer learning, offering a scalable foundation for robotics and autonomous systems to acquire versatile skills without relying on predefined reward signals.

name: Huayu Chen
paper_title: DiffusionNFT: Online Diffusion Reinforcement with Forward Process
synopsis: This research addresses the significant computational inefficiencies in training diffusion models with reinforcement learning caused by intractable likelihoods and rigid solver requirements. The authors introduce DiffusionNFT, a new online RL paradigm that optimizes image generation directly on the forward diffusion process using flow matching instead of storing complex sampling trajectories. Experiments show this approach is up to 25 times more efficient than current methods while achieving superior alignment scores without needing Classifier-Free Guidance. This advancement unlocks scalable post-training pipelines for visual AI, enabling companies to align high-quality generative models faster and with standard data solvers.

name: Huayu Chen
paper_title: InfoTok: Adaptive Discrete Video Tokenizer via Information-Theoretic Compression
synopsis: Current video tokenizers waste resources by applying fixed compression rates to all content, leading to unnecessary redundancy or quality loss on complex videos. To solve this, the researchers developed INFOTOK, an adaptive system that calculates optimal token counts based on each video's unique information complexity. Empirical results show the approach saves about 50% of tokens without sacrificing accuracy and achieves 2.3× better compression rates than existing adaptive techniques. This breakthrough enables significantly cheaper training and inference for long-form video models, unlocking scalable capabilities for upcoming multi-modal AI products.

name: Zhe Zhang
paper_title: DCFold: Efficient Protein Structure Generation with Single Forward Pass
synopsis: Current protein structure models like AlphaFold3 are highly accurate but prohibitively slow due to iterative calculations, which limits their use in high-throughput workflows such as drug discovery. DCFold addresses this by introducing a single-step generative model trained with a Dual Consistency framework that eliminates the multi-step diffusion bottleneck and uses a novel scheduling method. This approach delivers a fifteen-fold speedup in inference time while maintaining predictive accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art foundation models. Consequently, researchers can now perform rapid virtual screening and binder design tasks at scale without sacrificing the quality required for reliable biological applications.

name: Hao Zhou
paper_title: MemAgent: Reshaping Long-Context LLM with Multi-Conv RL-based Memory Agent
synopsis: This paper introduces MEMAGENT, a system that enables Large Language Models to analyze documents millions of tokens long without losing accuracy or slowing down processing speeds. Instead of trying to remember every detail, the model uses Reinforcement Learning to selectively update a fixed-length memory as it reads text in chunks, keeping computational costs efficient and linear. Experimental results show the method scales from an 8K context up to 3.5 million tokens with less than 10% performance loss, significantly outperforming other techniques that typically degrade on such lengths. This breakthrough unlocks practical applications like reading entire books or managing complex long-term agent tasks, making extremely scalable long-context processing viable for enterprise software.

name: Zijian Zhao
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
paper_title: Triple-BERT: Do We Really Need MARL for Order Dispatch on Ride-Sharing Platforms?
synopsis: Ride-sharing platforms face critical challenges matching drivers to passengers because traditional multi-agent AI models often struggle with scale and complex coordination. The researchers developed Triple-BERT, a new single-agent framework that uses efficient BERT-based networks to manage massive amounts of order data without excessive computational cost. Testing on real-world Manhattan data shows the system improves overall performance by nearly 12%, serving more orders and significantly cutting waiting times compared to current market-leading methods. This approach provides logistics companies with a scalable solution to optimize dispatch operations efficiently without needing to maintain complex, hard-to-train multi-agent architectures.

name: Roy Betser
institution: Technion - Israel Institute of Technology, Technion
paper_title: InfoNCE Induces Gaussian Distribution
synopsis: This research addresses the theoretical mystery behind why contrastive learning models trained with InfoNCE loss naturally produce feature embeddings that closely follow a Gaussian distribution. The authors demonstrate through mathematical analysis and experiments that these representations asymptotically converge to a multivariate Gaussian as dimensionality increases, especially when combined with light regularization for isotropy. This theoretical grounding allows practitioners to compute essential metrics like likelihood, entropy, and mutual information in closed form directly from the representation space. Consequently, companies can leverage these insights to enhance downstream tasks such as out-of-distribution detection, uncertainty estimation, and model calibration more reliably and efficiently.

name: Ang Lv
paper_title: Coupling Experts and Routers in Mixture-of-Experts via an Auxiliary Loss
synopsis: This paper addresses the common inefficiency in Mixture-of-Experts models where routers frequently misroute data because they lack a clear understanding of each expert's capabilities. The authors propose a lightweight auxiliary loss called ERC that mathematically forces routing decisions to align tightly with specialized expert functions without adding significant computational overhead. Experiments on large-scale models demonstrate that this method significantly improves performance and allows precise tracking of expert specialization during training. Ultimately, this research enables developers to build more scalable and efficient trillion-parameter AI systems by ensuring better alignment between routing logic and expert knowledge.

name: Pinyi Zhang
paper_title: P-GenRM: Personalized Generative Reward Model with Test-time User-based Scaling
synopsis: This research tackles the difficulty of aligning large language models to specific user preferences, particularly when feedback is sparse or user data is limited. The authors propose P-GenRM, a reward model that generates dynamic scoring criteria by clustering similar users and applying test-time scaling to reduce noise and improve accuracy. Experiments demonstrate the system achieves state-of-the-art performance by adapting effectively to unseen users without needing extensive training history. This capability opens significant opportunities for building personalized AI assistants and customer support bots that can instantly tailor interactions to diverse individual needs.
